APPENDIX

SUBCHAPTER  I

GENERAL  PROVISIONS

48.01
Title  and  legislative  purpose.  (i)  This  chapter  may
be  cited  as  ‘*  The  Children’s  Code’’.
This  chapter  shall  be
interpreted  to  effectuate  the  following  express  legislative
purposes:

(a)  To  provide  judicial  and  other  procedures  through
which  children  and  all  other  interested  parties  are  assured
fair  hearings  and  their  constitutional  and  other  legal
rights  are  recognized  and  enforced,  while  protecting  the
public  safety.

(b)  To  provide  for  the  care,  protection  and  whole-
some  mental  and  physical  development  of  children,  pre-
serving  the  unity  of  the  family  whenever  possible.

(c)  Consistent  with  the  protection  of  the  public  inter-

est,  to  remove  from  children  committing  delinquent  acts
the  consequences  of  criminal  behavior  and  to  substitute
therefore  a  program  of  supervision,  care  and  rehabilita-
tion.

(d)  To  divert  children  from  the  juvenile  justice  sys-
tem  to  the  extent  this  is  consistent  with  protection  of  chil-
dren  and  the  public  safety.

(e)  To  respond  to  children’s  needs  for  care  and  treat-
ment  through  community-based  programs  and  to  keep
children  in  their  homes  whenever  possible.

(f)  To  assure  that  children  pending  adoptive  homes
will  be  placed  in  the  best  homes  available  and  protected
from  adoption  by  persons  unfit  to  have  responsibility  for
raising  children.

App.  2

App.  3

(¢)  To  provide  children  in  the  state  with  permanent
and  stable  family  relationships.
The  courts  and  agencies
responsible  for  child  welfare  should  assist  parents  in
changing  any  circumstances  in  the  home  which  might  harm
the  child  or  which  may  require  the  child  to  be  placed  out-
side  the  home.

(2)

This  chapter  shall  be  liberally  construed  to  ef-
fect  the  objectives  contained  in  this  section.
The  best  in-
terests  of  the  child  shall  always  be  of  paramount  consider-
ation,  but  the  court  shall  also  consider  the  interest  of  the
parents  or  guardian  of  the  child,  the  interest  of  the  person
or  persons  with  whom  the  child  has  been  placed  for  adop-
tion  and  the  interests  of  the  public.

48.981
this  section:

Abused  or  neglected  children.  (1)  Derixirioxs.  In

(a)  **Abuse’*  means  any  physical  injury  inflicted  on
a  child  by  other  than  accidental  means,  or  sexual  inter-
course  or  sexual  contact  under  s.  940.225.
In  this  para-
graph,  ‘‘physical  injury’?  includes  but  is  not  limited  to
severe  bruising,  lacerations,  fractured  bones,  burns,  inter-
nal  injuries  or  any  injury  constituting  great  bodily  harin
under  s,  939.22  (14).

(b)  **Child’*  means  any  person  under  18  vears  of  age.

(c)  “County  agency’*

means  the  county  child  wel-

fare  agency  as  defined  in  s.  48.56  (1).

(d)  **Negleected  child’?  means  a  child  whose  parent,
guardian,  legal  custodian  or  other  person  exercising  temp-
orary  or  permanent  control  over  the  child  negleets,  re-
fuses  or  is  unable  for  reasons  other  than  poverty  to  pro-
vide  necessary  care,  food,  clothing,  medical  or  dental  care

or  shelter  so  as  to  seriously  endanger  the  physical  health
of  the  child.

(2)  PERSONS  REQUIRED  TO  REPORT  CASES  OF  SUSPECTED
A  physician,  coroner,  medical
CHILD  ABUSE  OR  NEGLECT.
examiner,  nurse,  dentist,  chiropractor,  opto  etrist,  or  any
other  medical  or  mental  health  professional,  social  or  pub-

lic  assistanee  worker,  school  teacher,  administrator  or
counselor,  child  care  worker  in  any  day  care  center  or

child  caring  institution  or  police  or  law  enforcement  of-
ricer  having  reasonable  cause  to  suspect  that  a  child  seen
in  the  course  of  professional  duties  has  been  abused  or

neglected  shall  report  as  provided  in  sub.  (3).  Any  other
person  having  reason  to  believe  that  a  child  has  been
abused  or  neglected  may  make  sue!  a  report.
No  person
making  a  report  under  this  subsection  may  be  discharged
from  employment  for  so  doing.

(3)  Procepures.

(a)  Initial  report.

Persons  re-
quired  to  report  under  sub.  (2)  shall  immediately  contact,
by  telephone  or  personally,  the  county  agency,  sheriff  or
city  police  department  and  shall  inform  the  agency  or  de-

partment  of  the  facts  and  circumstances  contributing  to  a
suspicion  of  child  abuse  or  neglect.  The  sheriff  or  police
department  shall  within  24  hours,  exclusive  of  Saturdays,
Sundays  or  legal  holidays,  refer  to  the  county  agency  all
cases  reported  to  it.  The  county  ageney  may  require  that
a  subsequent  report  be  made  in  writing.

(h)  Duties  of  local  law  enforcement  agencies.

1.  Any  person  reporting  suspected  abuse  or  neglect  of  a
child  may  request  an  immediate  investigation  by  the
sheriff  or  police  department  if  the  person  has  reason  to
believe  that  the  child’s  health  or  safety  is  in  immediate

App.  4

App.  0

danger.  Upon  receiving  such  a  request,  the  sheriff  or  po-
lice  departinent  shall  inunediately  investigate  to  deter-
mine  if  there  is  reason  to  believe  that  the  child’s  health
or  safecy  is  in  Immediate  danger  and  take  any  necessary
action  to  protect  the  child.

”.

If  the  investivatine  officer  has  reason  under  s.

48.19  (1)  (ce)  or  (d)  5  to  take  a  child  into  custody,  the  in-
vestigating  officer  shall  place  the  child  in  protective  cus-
tody  and  deliver  the  child  to  the  intake  worker  under  s.
48.20.

Le 3.

If  the  police  or  other  law  enforcement  officials
deiermine  that  criminal  action  is  necessary,  they  shall  re-
fer  the  case  to  the  district  attorney  for  criminal  prosecu-
tion.

(c)  Duties  of  county  agencies.

1,  The  county  agency
Within  24  hours  of
shall  act  in  accordance  with  s.  48.57.
the  receipt  of  an  initial  report  of  suspected  child  abuse  or
neglect,  the  county  agency  shall  commence  an  appropriate

and  thorough  investigation  to  determine  whether  the  re-
port  is  ‘‘indieated’*  or  ‘tunfounded’’.
The  complete  in-
vestigation  shall,  if  possible,  include  a  visit  to  the  child's
home  o«#  usual  place  of  abode,  observation  of  the  child  and
an  interview  with  the  child  and  the  child’s  parents  or  cus-

todians.

2.  Additionally,  within  72  hours  after  receipt  of  an
initial  report  the  county  agency  shall  complete  and  for-
ward  to  the  department  under  sub.  (7)  a  preliminary  in-
vestigative  report  containing  the  name,  address,  age  and
sex  of  each  child  involved  and  the  type  of  abuse  or  neglect
The  preliminary  report  shall  not  contain  any
suspected,
information  which  identifies  any  person  other  than  the
child.

3.  An  investigative  report  shall  be  classified  as  ‘*in-
A  finding  of  ‘‘indicated’’  for
dicated’’  or  ‘‘unfounded’’.
child  abuse  reports  shail  be  supported  by  a  preponderance
of the  evidence  available  to  the  agency;  a  finding  of  ‘*indi-
eated’*  for  negleet  reports  shall  be  supported  by  a  clear
and  convincing  preponderance  of  the  evidence  available  to
the  agency.
Whenever  there  is  less  than  the  required
standard  of  evidence  indicating  child  abuse  or  neglect,  the
report  shall  be  classified  as  ‘‘unfounded’’.
The  county
agency  director  or  his  or  her  designee  shall  review  and
approve  the  investigative  report  prior  to  transmitting  the
report  to  the  central  registry.  The  person  reviewing  and
approving  the  report  shall  not  have  participated  in  invest-
igating  the  suspected  abuse  or  neglect.  A  report  shall  not
be  classified  *‘indicated’*  solely  because  the  child’s  parent,
or  another  person  exercising  temporary  or  permanent  con-
trol  over  the  child’s  care,  in  good  faith  selecis  and  depends
upon  spiritual  means  or  prayer  for  treatment  or  cure  of
disease  or  for  remedial  care  of  the  child.  This  subdivision
does  not  preclude  a  court  from  ordering  that  medical  ser-
vices  be  provided  for  the  child,  if  the  child’s  health  re-
quires  it.  Reports  classified  as  **indicated’’  shall  include

a  description  of  the  services  being  provided  to  the  child
and  those  responsible  for  his  or  her  care,  as  well  as  all
relevant  dispositional  information,  and  shall  be  updated

at  6-month  intervals.
The  agency  shall  make  findings  as
required  under  this  subdivision  no  later  than  60  days
after  receipt  of  an  initial  report,  and  shall  immediately
transmit  the  investigative  report  to  the  central  registry

under  sub.  (8).

4.  After  transmitting  its  investigative  report  to  the
central  registry,  the  agency  shall  destroy  its  reports  con-

App.  6

App.  7

cerning  cases  of  child  abuse  or  neglect  classified  as  ‘‘un-

founded”’.

5.

If  the  person  investigating  on  behalf  of  the  county
agency  has  grounds  to  take  the  child  into  custody  under
s.  48.08(2)  or  48.19(1)(e),  the  person  shall  place  the  child
in  protective  eustody  and  shall  deliver  the  child  to  the  in-
take  worker  under  s.  48.20.

6.  The  county  ageney  shall  take  necessary  steps  to

protect  other  children  in  the  home.

7.  The  investigator  shall  inform  any  person  required
to  report  suspected  cases  of  child  abuse  or  neglect  that  the

report  was  unfounded  or  that  steps  were  taken  to  protect
the  health  and  welfare  of  a  child  who  is  the  subject  of  a  re-
port  made  by  the  person.
At  least  one  contact  shall  be
made  under  this  subdivision  within  60  days  after  receipt
of  the  initial  report  by  the  agency.

8.  The  county  agency  shall  cooperate  with  law  en-
forcement  officials,  courts  of  competent  jurisdiction  and
other  human  service  agencies  to  prevent,  identify  and  treat
child  abuse  and  neglect.  To  the  extent  possible,  the  county
agency  shall  coordinate  the  development  and  provision  of

services  to  children  found  to  be  abused  or  neglected  under

this  section.

9.  The  county  agency  shall  forward  a  copy  of  its

investigative  report  of  alleged  child  abuse  or  neglect  alleg-
ing  that  a  child’s  safety  is  endangered,  classified  as  *‘indi-
eated’’  or  *tunfounded’’,  to  the  central  registry  under

sub.  (8).

(d)  Indepenednt  investigation,

If  an  agent  of  an  in-
vestigating  agency  is the  subject  of  the  initial  report,  an  in-

dependent  investigation  shall  be  condueted  by  the  depart-
ment  or  by another  ageney  designated  by the  department,

(4)  ImMenNity  From  LiaBmory.

Any  person  or  institu-
tion  participating  in  good  faith  in  the  making  of  a  report,
ordering  or  taking  of  photographs  or  ordering  or  perform
ing  medical  examinations  of  a  child  under  this  section  shall
have  immunity  from  any  liability,  civil  or  criminal,  that  re-
sults  by  reason  of  the  action.
For  the  purpose  of  any  pro-
ceeding,  civil  or  criminal,  the  good  faith  of  any  person  re-
porting  under  this  section  shall  be  presumed.

(5)  Coroner’s  report.

Any  person  or  official

re-
quired  to  report  cases  of  suspected  child  abuse  or  negleet
who  has  reasonable  cause  to  suspect  that  a  child  died  as  a
result  of  child  abuse  or  neglect  shall  report  the  fact  to  the
appropriate  medical  examiner  or  coroner,  The  medical  ex-
aminer  or  coroner  shal!  accept  the  report  for  investigation
and  shall  report  the  findings  to  the  appropriate  district  at-
torney,  the  department,  the  county  agency  and,  if the  insti-
tution  making  their  report  initially  is a  hospital,  to  the  hos-

pital.

(6)  Penatty.  Whoever  wilfully  violates  this  section
by  failure  to  file  a  report  as  required,  may  be  fined  not
more  than  $100  or  imprisoned  not  more  than  6  months  or
both.

(7)  Five  oF  PRELIMINARY  REPORTS.

The  department
shall  maintain  preliminary  reports  filed  under  sub.  (38) (¢)2
in a  separate  file.  The  reports  shall  not  be  copied  or  dupli-
eated,  and  all  information  contained  therein  shall  be  con-
fidential  except  as  provided  in  this  subsection.
The  de-
partment  may,  in  response  to  an  inquiry  from  a  county

agency  about  a  specifie  child,  notify  the  ageney  only  that  a

Each

(8S)  Centra  recistry.  (a)  The  department  shall  main-
tain  a  confidential  statewide  central  registry  of  child  abuse

and  negleet  reports  made  under  sub.  (3)  (¢)  (3).  The  reg-
istry  shall  receive  and  maintain  written  reports  under  sub.
(3)  (ec)  3  from  county  agencies  of  suspected  child  abuse  or
neglect  cases  and  shall  transmit  information  to  authorized
persons  and  ageneies  under  sub.  (10).

(b)  The  central  registry  shall  be  operated  in  such  a

manner  as  to  enable  the  department  to:

1.  Provide  historical  information  to  aid  loeal  child
protective  service  agencies  in  determining  adequate  and
necessary  services  for  abused  children  and  their  families,
including  immediate  identification  and  location  of  prior  re-
ports  of child  abuse  and  neglect.

2.  Serve  as  a  case  management  tool  for  the  depart-
ment  in  reviewing  the  adequacy  of  local  child  protective  ef-
forts.

3.  Aid  research  efforts  aimed  at  increasing  the  gen-

eral  knowledge  of child  abuse  and  neglect.

(c)  Reports  of  child  abuse  and  neglect  shall  be  main-
un-

tained  at  the  central  registry  in  one  of  2 categories:
founded  or  indicated.

1  A  report  may  be  classified  as  ‘‘indicated’’  only
when  such  a  finding  is  supported  by  the  required  standard

App.  9

of  evidence  available  to  the  county  ageney,  as  provided  in

sub.  (3)  (¢e)  3.  Indicated  reports  may  be  maintained  in  the
central  registry  only  when  supplemental  information  as  re-
quired  under  sub.  (38)  (¢)  3 is attached.

2.  A  report  determined  by  the  county  agency  to  be
‘‘unfounded’’  shall  be  classified  ‘‘unfounded  by  reason  of
insufficient  evidence’’.

(d)  Investigative  reports  classified  as

‘‘indieated”’’
shall  contain  the  following  information:
the  names  and
addresses  of  the  child  and  the  child’s  parents  or  other  per-
sons  responsible  for  his  or  her  health  and  welfare;  the

child’s  age,  sex  and  race;  the  nature  and  extent  of  the

child’s  abuse  or  neglect,  including  any  evidence  of  prior  in-

juries,  abuse  or  neglect,  to  the  child  or  siblings;  the  names
of  the  person  alleged  to  be  responsible  for  the  abuse  or

neglect;  family  composition,  including  names,  ages,  sexes
and  races  of  other  children  in  the  home;  demographie  data
related  to  census  tract  data;  the  source  of  the  report;  the
person  making  the  report,  his  or  her  occupation,  and  where
the  reporter  can  be  reached;  and  the  medical,  legal  or  so-
cial  disposition,  including  a  description  of  any  services
provided.  The  names,  addresses  and  all  other  identifying
characteristics  of  persons  named  in  such  investigative  re-
ports  shall  not  be  placed  in  the  central  registry  until  the
requirements  of  sub.  (9)  are  met.

(e)  Investigative  reports  classified  as  ‘‘unfounded’’
shall  contain  all  the  information  required  under  par.  (d)
except  names,  addresses  and  other  identifying  characteris-
tics  of  the  persons  specified  in  par.  (d).

(f)  An  ‘‘indicated’’  report  and  all  names,  addresses
and  other  identifying  characteristics  placed  in  the  central

(11)  EpvcaTion,  TRAINING  AND  PROGRAM  DEVELOPMENT
\Np  COORDINATION.  (a)  The  department  and  county  agencies
to  the  extent  feasible  shall  conduct  continuing  education
and  training  programs  for  state  and  county  department
staff,  persons  and  officials  required  to  report,  the  general
public  and  others  as  appropriate.  The  programs  shall  be
designed  to  encourage  reporting  of child  abuse  and  neglect,
to  encourage  self-reporting  and  voluntary  acceptance  of
services  and  to  improve  communication,  cooperation  and
coordination  among  all  agencies  in  the  identification,  pre-
vention  and  treatment  of  child  abuse  and  neglect.  The  de-
partment  and  county  agencies  shall  develop  public  infor-
mation  programs  about  child  abuse  and  neglect.

(b)  The  department  shall  to  the  extent  feasible  en-
cure  that  there  are  available  in  the  state  adininistrative
procedures,  personnel  trained  in  child  abuse  and  neglect,

App.  14

multidisciplinary  programs  and  operational  procedures
and  capabilities  to  deal  effectively  with  child  abuse  and
‘These  procedures  and  capabilities  may  in-
neglect  cases.
clude,  but  are  not  limited  to,  receipt,  investigation  and  ver-
ification  of  reports;  determination  of  treatment  or  amelio-
rative  social  services;  or  referral  to  the  appropriate  court.

(c)  In  meeting  its  responsibilities  under  par.  (a)  or
(b),  the  department  or  a  county  agency  may  contract  with
any  public  or  private  organization  which  meets  the  stan-
In  entering  into  the  con-
dards  set  by  the  department.
tracts  the  department  or  county  ageney  shall  give  priority
to  parental  organizations  combating  child  abuse  and  ne-
vlect.

(12)  Anxvuan  revorts.

No  later  than  August  1  of
each  vear  the  department  shall  prepare  and  transmit  to
the  governor  and  the  legislature  a  report  on  the  status  of
The  report  shall  in-
child  abuse  and  neglect  programs.
clude  a  full  statistical  analvsis  of  the  child  abuse  and  ne-
elect  reports  made  through  tie  last  calendar  year,  an  eval-
uaiion  of  services  offered  under  this  section  and  their  ef-
fectiveness,  and  recommendations  for  additional  legislative
and  other  action  to  fulfill  the  purpose  of  this  section.  The
department  shall  provide  statistical  breakdowns  by county,
if  requested  by  a  county.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

1.  Whether  a  due  process  violation  can  be  based  on  a
County’s  failure  to  prevent  physical  abuse  inflicted  by  a
natural  father  on  his  child  when  the  County’s  sole  in-
volvement  in  the  situation  is  that  it  had  been  providing
social  services  to  the  family  in  question  on  a  voluntary
basis?

2.  Whether  county  officials  can  be  found  to  have  acted
with  the  intent  necessary  to  establish  a  due  process
violation  when,  in  the  course  of  providing  voluntary
social  services  to  a  family,  the  officials  concluded  that,
in  their  professional  judgment,  there  was  an  insufficient
statutory  basis  to  seek  judicial  intervention  which,  in
turn,  may  have  led  to  the  termination  of  a  natural  par-
ent’s  constitutionally  protected  custodial  rights?

3.  Whether  a  County  and  its  Department  of  Social
Services  can  be  held  liable  in  a  Section  1983  action  where
the  County’s  express  policy  is to  attempt  to protect  against
the  injury  that  occurred  and  where  the  employees  who
allegedly  failed  to  prevent  that  injury  lacked  policy-
making  authority  under  state  law?

4.  Whether  county  employees  are  entitled  to  a  quali-
fied  immunity  in  a  Section  1983  action  where  the  con-
stitutional  violation  that  they  are  charged  with  would
be  recognized  for  the  first  time  by  this  Court  in  the
case  involving  those  employees  and  where  the  only  rele-
vant,  established  constitutional  rights  at  the  time  the
employees  acted  were  those  of  a  natural  father  in  not
having  his
with
improperly
terminated?

IN  THE
Supreme  Court  of the United  States
OCTOBER  TERM,  1988

No.  87-154

JOSHUA  DESHANEY,  a  Minor,  by his  Guardian
ad  litem,  and  MELODY  DESHANEY,

Petitioners,

v.

WINNEBAGO  COUNTY  DEPARTMENT  OF  SOCIAL
SERVICES;  WINNEBAGO  COUNTY;  ANN  KEMMETER
and  CHERYL  STELSE,

Respondents.

On  Writ  of Certiorari  to the  United  States
Court  of Appeals  for the  Seventh  Circuit

BRIEF  FOR  RESPONDENTS

STATEMENT

Respondent  Ann  Kemmeter,  a  social  worker  with  the
Winnebago  County  Department  of  Social  Services,  first
came  into  contact  with  Joshua  DeShaney  on  January  22,
1983.  (J.A.  95.)  On  that  date,  Dr.  Gehringer,  a  pedia-
trician  at  the  Theda  Clark  Regional  Medical  Center,
examined  Joshua’s  multiple  contusions  and  recommended
that  the  child  be  admitted  to  the  hospital  because  of  “pos-
sible”  child  abuse.  (J.A.  87,  95.)  The  hospital  then  con-

2

3

tacted  the  Department,  which  in  turn  requested  that  an
intake  worker  at  the  Winnebago  County  Circuit  Court
place  temporary  custody  of  Joshua  with  the  hospital.
(J.A.  88,  96.)  Pursuant  to  the  provisions  of  § 48.025
and  § 48.207,  Wis.  Stats.,  the  request  was  granted.  (Jd.)

Three  days  later,  the  hospital  convened  a  child  pro-
(J.A.  88,  96.)  '
tection  team  to  consider  Joshua’s  case.
After  the  team  discussed  the  child’s  condition  in  detail,
the  assistant  corporation  counsel—charged  by  Wisconsin
law  with  commencing  any  court  action  in  cases  of  sus-
pected  child  abuse,  see  § 48.09,  Wis.  Stats.—indicated  that
there  was  insufficient  evidence  to  take  the  matter  into
Juvenile  Court.  (J.A.  89,  93,  97.)  Subsequently,  as  re-
quired  by  § 48.981  (3)  (c)  (3),  Wis.  Stats.,  the  Department
filed  a  report  with  the  State  of  Wisconsin  concluding  that
the suspicion  ot abuse  was  “unfounded.”  (J.A.  89,97.)

Based  on  the  recommendation  of  the  Theda  Clark
child  protection  team—and  as  mandated  by  Wisconsin
law  *—-custody  of  Joshua  was  returned  to  his  father,
114.)
Randy  DeShaney,  on  January  25,  1983.
On  February  14,  1983,  the  Winnebago  County  Juvenile

(J.A.

1  The  team  included  Dr.  Gehringer,  Dr.  Derozier  (a  clinical  psy-
chologist  who  had  examined  Joshua  DeShaney),  Tom  Hoare  (the
hospital  social  worker),  Respondent  Ann  Kemmeter,  Respondent
Chery]  Stelse  (Ann  Kemmeter’s  suprevisor),  John  Bodnar  ( Assistant
Corporation  Counsel  for  Winnebago  County),  an”  Keith  Nelson
(Neenah  Police  Department  detective),  along  with  various  other
hospital  personnel.  (J.A.  89,  96-97.)

2  The  Wisconsin  statutory  scheme  required  the  return  of  Joshua
to  his  natural  father  “as  soon  as  is  reasonably  possible.”  See  gen-
erally  $$ 48.20,  48.21,  Wis.  Stats.  The  hospital’s  temporary  phy-
sical  custody  could  only  have  been  maintained  if  a  petition  initiat-
ing  proceedings  under  Chapter  48  of  the  Wisconsin  Statutes  had
been  filed  by  a  “district  attorney,  city  attorney  or  corporation  coun-
sel  or  other  appropriate  officials  specified  under  s.  48.09.”
If  a
hearing  is  not  held  within  forty-eight  hours,  the  “juvenile  court
Commission  shall  order  the  child’s  immediate  release  from  cus-
tody.”  § 48.21(1)(b),  Wis.  Stats.

Intake  Court  closed  the  child  protective  case;  thereafter,
the  County’s  involvement  with  the  DeShaney  family  was
on  a  voluntary  “family  services”  basis.
(J.A.  90,  97-98.)

In  particular,  between  February  14,  1983  and  March
8,  1984,  Ann  Kemmeter  continued  to  work  with  the
DeShaney  family.*  The  family  entered  into  a  voluntary
and  non-binding  “social  services  agreement”  with  the
Department,  specifying  the  services  to  be  afforded  as
“Individual  and  Family  Adjustment,”  “Education,”  “Em-
ployment,”  and  “Health.”*  Respondent  Kemmeter  thus
focused  her  efforts  on  proper  parenting  and  appropriate
disciplinary  techniques,  and  she  observed  the  family
setting  when  circumstances  permitted.  (J.A.  99.)  Dur-
ing  her  thirteen  months  of  working  with  the  DeShaney
family,  she  made  approximately  twelve  home  visits  and
attempted  five  more.  (J.A.  98, 99.)5

Pursuant  to  the  procedures  of  the  Department  of  So-
cial  Services,  Respondent  Kemmeter  maintained  an  on-
going  case  narrative  relating  to  her  involvement  with  the
DeShaney  family,  which  is  reproduced  in  the  Joint  Ap-
pendix  at  110-46.
In  this  narrative,  she  expressed  her
“eoncern”  and  “suspicion”  related  to  the  possibility  of
child  abuse  occurring  within  the  DeShaney  family,  but
also  felt  that  “{n]one  of  this  can  be  proven,  and  at  this

*  It was  the  practice  of  the  Department  to  keep  a  specific  volun-
tary  family  services  case  assigned  to  the  worker  that  was  originally
involved  with  the  case  in  the  child  protection  services  phase,  even
after  the  original  referral  had  been  deemed  “unfounded.”

*  Judge  Posner  in  the  decision  below  incorrectly  assumed  that  the
written  agreement  was  entered  into  pursuant  to  the  provisions  of

36-37.  Because  the  agreement  with  Mr.  DeShaney  was  not  entered
into  by  an  “intake  worker”  as  part  of  an  “informal  disposition,”
however,  § 48.245,  Wis.  Stats.  is  inapplicable  by  its  terms.

5It  was  Department  policy  for  a  caseworker  to  make  one  at-
tempted  home  visit  per  month  on  any  open  family  services  case.
(J.A.  90.)

point  of time,  none  of them  [i.e.,  the  injuries}  are  serious

ents  Kemmeter  and  Stelse  ever  feel  that  there  was  suffi-
cient  evidence  or  facts  pertaining  to  Joshua  to  take  the

to court  in  an  attempt  to  remove  the  child  from  his natural
father.

quently  took  custody  of Joshua;  his  father  was  prosecuted
for and  convicted  of child  abuse.

brought  suit  against  Respondents  in  the  District  Court

sembled  by  the  Winnebago  County  Department  of  Social  Services

tution  and  42  U.S.C.  § 1983.*

ish  the  Head  Start  worker  and  advised  that  it  would  have  been

‘s  repeatedly  described  as  cigarette  burns”  on  Joshua’s  face.  After
«  home  visit  on  November  9,  1983,  however,  Kemmeter  indicated

dered”  if  they  might  have  been  cigarette  burns.  When  the  parents

erence  to  the  record,  that  the  “Respondents  received  no  fewer  than

cials  who  treated  Joshua  notified  the  State  Protection  System  that
Joshua  was  being  abused.”

8  Melody  DeShaney  is  Joshua’s  natural  mother.

proceedings  between  Kandy  and  Melody,  a  Wyoming  court  trans-

and  lives  in a  group  home.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

This  case  involves  a  Section  1983  claim  for  damages
based  upon  the  alleged  failure  of  a  County  and  its
employees  to  protect  a  child  from  abuse  inflicted  by
Petitioners’  claim  is  barred  for  several  in-
his  father.
dependent  legal  reasons,  each  of  which  requires  affirm-
ance  of the  judgment  below.

1.  Petitioners  have  failed  to  show  any  constitutional
deprivation  by a  state  actor,  an  essential  prerequisite  in
a  Section  1983  case  based  on  an  alleged  violation  of  the
Due  Process  Clause.  The  harm  to  Joshua  DeShaney  was
inflicted  by  his  father,  whose  actions  were  not  merely
independent  of  any  governmental  entity  or  person,  but
in  flat  derogation  of  government  policy,  as  Petitioners
themselves  concede.  Pet.  Br.  at  19.  To  fill  this  fatal  void,
Petitioners  attempt  to  fashion  a  new  constitutional  duty,
supposedly  based  on  a  “special  relationship’—.e.,  that
the  Respondent  County  had  undertaken  to  provide  child
protection  services  and  that  the  government  officials  per-
forming  these  services  were  aware  of  Petitioner’s  poten-
tial  plight  but  failed  to  protect  him.  This  effort  to  trans-
form  a  privately  inflicted  harm  into  a  governmental
deprivation  stretches  the  Due  Process  Clause  well  be-
yond  its breaking  point.

Petitioners  recognize,  as  they  must,  that  “there  is  no
generalized  duty  on  a  state  or  municipality  to  provide
affirmative  protection  to  members  of  the  public  at  large.”
Pet.  Br.  at  12-13.  Nor  is  there  any  warrant  for  requir-
ing  a  state  or  county  that  wishes  to  provide  family
services  to  do  su  ..  its  constitutional  peril.  Whether  those

8

9

services  ultimately  accomplish  their  goal  will  depend  on
a  variety  of  factors,  many  of  which  are  clearly  beyond
Moreover,  the  effi-
the  government’s  power  to  control.
eacy  of  any  such  governmental  program  necessarily  de-
pends  in  part  on  the  availability  of  funding  and  thus
implicates  complicated  allocative  decisions  that  cut  across
the  spectrum  of  public  services.
In  these  circumstances,
the  government’s  inability  to  achieve  perfection  simply
cannot  be  equated  with  the  kind  of  “affirmative  abuse  of
power”  needed  to establish  a due process  violation.  Daniels
v.  Williams,  474  U.S.  327,  330  (1986)  (quoting  Parratt
v.  Taylor,  451  U.S.  527,  549  (1981)
(Powell,  J.,  con-
curring)  ).

The  cases  relied  on  by  Petitioners  and  their  supporting
The  government  here
amici  are  simply  inapposite.
neither  inflicted  the  injury,  see,  e.g.,  Ingraham  v.  Wright,
430  U.S.  651  (1977),  nor  otherwise  deprived  Petitioner
of  his  liberty,  thereby  creating  ancillary  constitutional
obligations  to  provide  affirmative  services  like  medical
care  or  a  safe  environment.
See,  e.g.,  Youngberg  v.
Romeo,  457  U.S.  307  (1982);  Estelle  v.  Gamble,  429
U.S.  97  (1976).  The  government’s  action  in  those  cases
—either  through  direct  infliction  or  through  an  initial
deprivation  of  liberty  that  both  renders  an  individual
wholly  dependent  upon  the  government  for  protection
and  provides  the  government  the  degree  of  control  neces-
sary  for  it  to  afford  reasonably  effective  protection—
is  properly  viewed  as  sufficient  to  make  out  a  “dep-
Daniels,  supra,
rivation  in  the  constitutional  sense.”
474  U.S.  at  329  (quoting  Parratt,  supra,  451  U.S.  at  548
But
(Powell,  J.,  concurring) ) (emphasis  in  original).
where  all  that  the  government  has  done  is  to  provide
voluntary  family  counseling  in  a  case  that  involves  a
privatey  inflicted  injury,  the  resulting  harm  is  “too
remote  a  consequence”  of  governmental  action  to  make
out  such  a  deprivation.
Martinez,  supra,  444  U.S.  at
285.

2.  Even  assuming  that  Petitioners  had  properly  al-
leged  a  constitutional  deprivation,  their  claims  would
still  be  barred  because  they  do  not,  and  could  not,  show
that  Respondents  acted  with  the  requisite  “state  of  mind”
for  a  due  process  violation.
The  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment  constrains  only  “real  abuses  by  state  officials  in
the  exercise  of  governmental  powers,”  Parratt,  supra,
451  U.S.  at  549  (Powell,  J.,  concurring)  (emphasis  in
original),  and  therefore  demands  a  high  level  of  intent
before  liability  may  be  imposed.  The  appropriate  stand-
ard  in  a  case  like  this  one—which  involves  a  claim  of
government  inaction  and  requires  the  balancing  of  a
child’s  potential  rights  against  those  of  his  family—
should  be  the  “deliberate  indifference”  test.  See  Estelle,
supra.  The  alternatives  proposed  by  petitioners—gross
negligence  or  recklessness—readily  blur  into  the  negli-
gence  standard  previously  rejected  by  this  Court,  see
Daniels,  supra,  474  U.S.  at  330;  Davidson  v.  Cannon,  474
U.S.  344  (1986),  and  simply  do  not  reflect  the  kind  of
“deliberate  decisions  of  government  officials”  required  by
the  Due  Process  Clause.  Daniels,  supra,  474  U.S.  at  331
(emphasis  in  original)  (citations  omitted).

In  the  present  case,  it  cannot  plausibly  be  claimed
that  Respondents  acted  with  deliberate  indifference  to-
ward  any  rights  that  Petitioner  may  have  had.  On  the
contrary,  for  more  than  a  year  they  provided  voluntary
counseling  to  the  DeShaney  family.
In  that  capacity,
while  remaining  attentive  to  the  child’s  situation,  Re-
spondents  also  had  to  be  respectful  of  the  constitutional
rights  of  a  natural  parent  regarding  the  privacy  and
See,  e.g.,  Stanley  v.  Illinois,
integrity  of  family  life.
405  U.S.  645  (1972).  With  the  benefit  of  hindsight,  it
may  now  be  said  that  a  tragedy  occurred.
But  that
tragedy  was  hardly  the  product  of  any  purposeful  or  de-
liberate  indifference  on  the  part  of  Respondents.
See
Davidson,  supra,  474  U.S.  at  348.

10

3.  The  County  and  its  Department  of  Social  Services
must  be  dismissed  for  the  separate  reason  that  Pe-
titioners  can  point  to  no  policy  or  custom  directly  lead-
ing  to  the  alleged  violation  of  their  rights.  Monell  v.  De-
partment  of  Social  Services,  436  U.S.  658  (1978);  Polk
County  v.  Dodson,  454  U.S.  312,  326  (1981).  On  the
contrary,  even  Petitioners  have  acknowledged  that  “the
state  and  county  had  expressed  specific  policies  and
intentions  to  seek  out  and  prevent  child  abuse.”  Pet.  Br.
at  19.  (emphasis  supplied).  In  the  face  of  this  recogni-
tion,  the  highly  discretionary  decisions  of  front-line  so-
cial  workers—who  lacked  any  policymaking  authority
under  state  law  (§ 46.22,  Wis.  Stats.)——provide  no  basis
for  holding  the  county  Respondents  liable.  See  City  of
St.  Louis  v.  Praprotnik,  108  S.  Ct.  915  (1988)  (plurality
opinion) ; Oklahoma  City  v.  Tuttle,  471  U.S.  808  (1985)
(plurality  opinion).

4.  Finally,  the  individual  Respondents  in  this  case
are  further  protected  from  liability  on  the  basis  of  the
qualified  immunity  doctrine.
Under  that  doctrine,  an
official  cannot  be  held  responsible  in  a  damages  action
unless  it  can  be  shown  that  he  or  she  violated  a  “clearly
established  statutory  or  constitutional  right  of  which
a  reasonable  person  would  have  known.”
Harlow  v.
Fitzgerald,  457  U.S.  800  (1982).  The  right  at  issue  in
this  case  was  anything  but  clearly  established,  as  the
decisions  by  both  courts  below  and  numerous  others
amply  demonstrate.

11

ARGUMENT

I.  THERE  IS  NO  CONSTITUTIONAL  VIOLATION  IN
THIS  CASE  BECAUSE  THE  WINNEBAGO  COUNTY
DEPARTMENT  OF  SOCIAL  SERVICES  DID  NOT
DEPRIVE  PETITIONERS  OF  THEIR  LIBERTY.

To  state  a  claim  under  Section  1983,  a  plaintiff  must
both  allege  the  violation  of  a  right  secured  by the  Consti-
tution  and  laws  of  the  United  States  and  show  that  the
alleged  deprivation  was  committed  by a  person  acting  un-
der  color  of  state  law.  West  v.  Atkins,  108  S.  Ct.  2250,
2255-56  (1988).  The  Court  has  thus  instructed  that  “the
first  inquiry  in  any  § 1983  suit  .
.  is whether  the  plaintiff
has  been  deprived  of  a  right  ‘secured  by  the  Constitution
and  laws.’”  Baker  v.  McCollan,  443  U.S.  137,  140  (1979)
(quoting  Section  1983).

.

But  the  identification

Petitioner  has  stumbled  at  this  threshold.  Rather  than
demonstrating  that  Respondents  have  deprived  him  of
a  protected  interest,  he  is  able  only  to  indicate  that  the
Constitution  accords  him  a  liberty  interest  in  personal
security.
of  a  constitutionally
protected  interest  constitutes  only  half  of  a  Section  1983
claim.  The  Due  Process  Clause  does  not  protect  citizens
against  harms  inflicted  by  private  parties,  but  only
against  deprivations  of  individual  liberties  at  the  hands
of  the  government.  Petitioner  has  failed  to  demonstrate
that  it  was  Winnebago  County  or  its  employees  who
deprived  him  of  the  constitutionally  protected  interest
that  he asserts.

A.  The  Constitution  Imposes  No  General  Duty  On  The
Government  To  Protect  Its  Citizens  From  Private
Violence.

The  injuries  for  which  Joshua  DeShaney  seeks  redress
were  all  inflicted  by  his  father  while  Petitioner  was  liv-
ing  at  home.  Petitioner  is  thus  in  the  same  position  as
any  other  citizen  who  is  the  unfortunate  victim  of  crime.
His  injury  was  not  inflicted  by  the  State,  nor  did  it

12

occur  at  a  time  when  the  State  had  deprived  him  of  his
liberty  or  otherwise  burdened  his  constitutional  rights.
Because  the  Due  Process  Clause  imposes  no  obligation  to
protect  a  citizen  at  liberty  from  harms  committed  by
private  parties,  Petitioner  has  failed  to  state  a  claim
under  Section  1983.

Petitioner  virtually  concedes  that  his  claim  falls  out-
side  the  purview  of  the  Constitution  when  he  recognizes,
as  he  must,  that  “there  is  no  generalized  duty  on  a  state
or  municipality  to  provide  protection  to  members  of  the
public  at  large.”
(citing  Martinez,
Pet.  Br.  at  12-13
supra).  See  Youngberg,  supra,  457  U.S.  at  317  (“As  a
general  matter,  a  State  is  under  no  constitutional  duty
to  provide  substantive  services  for  those  within  its  bor-
Although  the  State  may  not  itself  invade  an
der.”).
individual’s  personal  security,  it  is  not  compelled  by  the
Constitution  to  prevent  private  action  from  having  the
same  effect.

The  Court  has  thus  repeatedly  held  that  the  Constitu-
tion  does  not  provide  an  affirmative  right  to  govern-
mental  aid,  even  in  the  pursuit  of  those  interests  that
the  government  may  not  itself  deprive.  For  example,  in
Harris  v.  McRae,  448  U.S.  297  (1980),  the  Court  ex-
plicitly  rejected  the  argument  that  “because  government
may  not  prohibit  the  use  of  contraceptives,  or  prevent
parents  from  sending  their  children  to  a  private  school,
government,  therefore,  has  an  affirmative  constitutional
obligation  to  ensure  that  all  persons  have  the  financial
resources  to  obtain  contraceptives  or  to  send  their  chil-
dren  to  private  schools.”  Jd.  at  318  (citations  omitted).
(no
See  also  Maher  v.  Roe,  432  U.S.  464,  469  (1977)
constitutional  right  to  public  funding  of  abortion)  ; Lind-
sey  v.  Normet,  405  U.S.  56,  74  (1972)  (same  for  hous-
ing).

The  Court  has  also  specifically  indicated  that  there  is
no  affirmative  constitutional  obligation  on  the  govern-
ment  to  provide  protective  services.  In  Turner  v.  United

13

States,  284  U.S.  354,  358  (1919),  for  example,  in  an
opinion  by  Justice  Brandeis,  the  Court  denied  a  claim
for  compensation  arising  from  damages  caused  by  mob
violence,  noting  the  “lack  of  a  substantive  right  to  re-
cover  the  damages  resulting  from  failure  of  a  govern-
ment  or  its  officers  to  keep  the  peace.””’  The  same  prin-
ciple  underlies  the  more  recent  decision  in  National  Board
of  Y.M.C.A.  v.  United  States,  369  U.S.  85  (1969),  where
a  Fifth  Amendment  Takings  Clause  claim  was  denied
for  the  damage  done  to  buildings  under  protective  oc-
cupation  during  a  riot  in  the  Panama  Canal  zone.  As
Justice  Harlan  wrote  in  concurrence,  “[e] very  man  who
was  robbed  on  the  street  cannot  demand  compensation
from  the  government  on  the  ground  that  the  Fifth
Amendment  requires  fully  effective  police  protection  at
all  times.”  Jd.  at  95."

As  these  cases  demonstrate,  a  plaintiff  cannot  avoid
the  constitutional  requirement  of  state,  rather  thn  pri-
vate,  action  merely  by  rephrasing  his  Complaint  to  at-

.

10  Not  surprisingly,  the  Courts  of  Appeals  are  in  unanimous
agreement  that  the  Constitution  imposes  no  general  duty  on  the
States  to  provide  protective  services.
In  Estate  of  Gilmore  v.
Buckley,  787  F.2d  714,  720  (1st  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  107  S.  Ct.
Judge  Campbell  concluded  that  “[n]othing  in  the  four-
270  (1986),
.  suggests  that  it  was  written
teenth  amendment  or  its  history  .
to  provide  an  expansive  guarantee  of  State  protective  services.”
Judge  Posner,  in  addition  to  the  opinion  below,  has  stated  that
there  is “no  constitutional  right  to  be  protected  by the  State  against
.  criminals  or  madmen,”  and  in  corollary,  because  there  is  no
“constitutional  duty  [on  the  State]  to  provide  such  protection  its
failure  to  do  so  is  not  actionable  under  Section  1983.”  Bowers  v.
DeVito,  686  F.2d  616,  618  (7th  Cir.  1982).  The  same  point  was
later  amplified  by  Judge  Easterbrook  in  Archie  v.  City  of  Racine,
847  F.2d  1211  (7th  Cir.  1987)  (en  banc).  See  also  Wideman  v.
Shallowford  Community  Hosp.,
1033-34
(llth  Cir.  1987);  Ketchum  v.  Alameda  County,  811  F.2d  1243
(9th  Cir.  1987);  Janan  v.  Trammell,  785  F.2d  557,  560  (6th  Cir.
1986);  Fox  v.  Custis,  712  F.2d  84,  88  (4th  Cir.  1983);  Humann  v.
Wilson,  696  F.2d  783,  784  (10th  Cir.  1983).

1030,

F.2d

Inc.,

826

14

15

failures

tack  official
to  prevent  private  deprivations.
Allowing  that  kind  of  broad  expansion  of  due  process
protection  would  not  only  do  violence  to  the  text  of  the
Clause,  it  would  also  drastically  alter  the  role  of  the
Constitution  in  ordering  governmental  action.
As  the
Court  has  explained:

Careful  adherence  to  the  ‘state  action’  requirement
preserves  an  area  of  individual  freedom  by  limiting
the  reach  of  federal  law  and  Federal  judicial  power.
It  also  avoids  imposing  on  the  State,  its  agencies  or
officials,  responsibility  for  conduct  for  which  they
cannot  fairly  be  blamed.  A  major  consequence  is  to
require  the  courts  to  respect  the  limits  of  their  own
power  as  directed  against  state  governments  and
private  interests.  Whether  this  is  good  or  bad  pol-
icy,  it  is  a  fundamental  fact  of  our  political  order.

Lugar  v,  Edmondson  Oil  Co.,  457  U.S.  922,  936-37  (1982)
(emphasis  supplied).

In  sum,  far  from  being  an  instrument  to  ensure  that
States  act  to  protect  their  citizens,  the  Due  Process
Clause  was  intended  to  prohibit  “real  abuses  by  state
officials  in  the  exercise  of  governmental  powers.”  Par-
ratt,  supra,  451  U.S.  at  549  (Powell,  J.,  concurring)
(emphasis  in  original)  ; see  also  Daniels,  supra,  474  U.S.
at  327;  Davidson,  supra,  474  U.S.  at  344,  347;  Parratt,
supra,  451  U.S.  544-45  (Stewart,  J.,  concurring).

B.  There  Is  Insufficient  Government  Action  In  This
Case  To  Transform  Petitioners’  Private  Injury  Into
a  Constitutional  Deprivation.

In  lieu  of  a  general  claim  that  a  lack  of  protection
deprived  them  of  liberty,  Petitioners  attempt  to  narrow
their  position  by  arguing  that  a  duty  “arise[s]  out  of
the  relationship  created  or  undertaken  by  the  state  with
respect  to  certain  parties.”
Pet.  Br.  at  13.  While  we
agree  that  such  a  duty  may  be  created  by  certain  kinds
of  state  action,  the  requisite  state  involvement  is  not
present  in  this  case.  On  the  contrary,  a  constitutional

duty  to  provide  protection  against  private  harm  can  only
arise  in  circumstances  where  the  government,_by_its own
action,  has  deprived  an  individual  of  his  or  her  liberty.
In that  circumstance,  the  State  may  have  affirmatively  ren-
dered  the  individual  wholly  dependent  upon  it  for  basic
protection  and,  by reason  of such  deprivation,  assumed  the
degree  of  control  that  enables  it  to  provide  reasonably  ef-
fective  protection.

1.  The  General  Requirement  That  The  State  Must
Actively  Inflict  Injury  Before  It  May  Be  Held
Liable  For  A  Constitutional  Violation  Flows  Di-
rectly  From  The  Due  Process  Clause  As  Well  As
The  Provisions  Of  Section  1983.

Under  both  the  “state  action”  requirement  of  the  Due
Process  Clause  and  the  “causation”  requirement  of  Sec-
tion  1983,  the  government  cannot  be  held  liable  for  harm
to  a  citizen  at  liberty  unless  it  actively  inflicts  the  in-
jury.  Even  though  the  government  may  seek  to  prevent
the  very  harm  that  results  from  third-party  actions,  its
efforts  in  that  regard  are  insufficient  to  justify  the  im-
position  of  constitutional  liability.  Rather,  in  such  cir-
cumstances,  the  injury  for  which  the  Plaintiff  seeks
redress  is  simply  “too  remote  a  consequence  of | official’s |
actions  to  hold  them  responsible  under  the  federal  civil
rights  laws.”’  Martinez,  supra,  444  U.S.  at  285.

This  Court’s  proscription  against  attempting  to  “con-
stitutionalize”  such  “remote  consequences  of  official  ac-
tion”  was  firmly  set  forth  in  Martinez,  a  case  involving  a
homicide  committed  by a  parolee  five  months  after  his  re-
lease.  Noting  that  “it  is  perfectly  clear  that  not  every  in-
jury  in  which  a  State  official  has  played  some  part  is  ac-
tionable  under  [Section  1983],”  the  Court  explained:

Although  the  decision  to  release  Thomas  from  prison
was  action  by  the  State,  the  action  of  Thomas  five
months  later  cannot  be  fairly  characterized  as  state
action.  Regardless  of  whether,  as  a  matter  of  State

16

17

tort  law,  the  parole  board  could  be  said  either  to
have  had  a  ‘duty’  to  avoid  harm  to  his  victim  or  to
have  proximately  caused  her  death,  we  hold  that,
taking  these  particular  allegations  as  true,  appellees
did  not  ‘deprive’  appellants’  decedent  of  life  within
the  meaning  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.

Id.  at  284-85  (citations  omitted).

This  conclusion  is  fully  consistent  with  the  Court’s
general  jurisprudence  in  the  “state  action”  area.  Indeed,
that  requirement  is  specifically  designed  to  separate
constitutional  wrongs  from  private  ones.  Although  the
inquiry  essentially  involves  a  case-by-case,  fact-bound
determination,  it  is  nonetheless  clear  that  private  action
may  be  attributed  to  the  State  only  when  the  private
ends  were  accomplished  “with  the  overt,  significant  as-
sistance  of  state  officials.”
Tulsa  Professional  Collection
Services,  Inc.  v.  Pope,  108  S.  Ct.  1340,  1845  (1988)."

Applying  the  factors  that  are  generally  considered  in
determining  whether  there  has  been  state  action,  it  is
apparent  that  none  of  the  traditional  indicia  is  present
here.  For  example,  child  abuse  is  hardly  “the  exclusive
prerogative  of  the  State,”  Blum  v.  Yaretsky,  457  U.S.
991,  1011  (1982),  and  Respondents’  attempts  to  inter-
vene  in  Joshua’s  situation  belie  any  assertion  that  his
father  was  a  “willful  participant  in  joint  activity  with
the  State  or  its  agents,”  United  States  v.  Price,  383  U.S.
787,  794  (1966),  or  that  there  was  a  “symbiotic  relation-
ship”  between  him  and  the  County  or  any  of  its  agents.
Burton  v.  Wilmington  Parking  Authority,  365  U.S.  715

11  Compare  Flagg  Bros.,  Inc.  v.  Brooks,  436  U.S.  149  (1978)  (no
State  action  in  private  creditor’s  lawful  self-help  pursuant  to  the
Uniform  Commercial  Code)  with  Lugar,  supra,  (ministerial  State
involvement  implicates  State  action  in  private  creditor’s  collec-
tion  action)  and  North  Georgia  Finishing  Co.  v.  Di-Chem,  Inc.,  419
U.S.  601  (1975)  (same)  and  Sniadach  v.  Family  Finance  Corp.,  395
U.S.  337  (1969)  (same).

(1961).  Rather,  as  the  course  of  the  County’s  investiga-
tion  demonstrates,  Mr.  DeShaney  was  a  suspect  for  child
abuse—he  was  thus  “not  acting  on  behalf  of  the  State;
he  [was]  the  State’s  adversary.”  Dodson,  supra,  454  U.S.
at 323  n.13.

action,

The  constitutional

requirement  of  state

in
short,  is not  met  when  the  government’s  efforts  at  provid-
ing  protection,  even  if  ultimately  ineffective,  do  not  ren-
der  the  intended  beneficiary  any  worse  off.  As  Justice
Harlan  explained  in  an  analogous  situation,  the  proper
standard  for  allowing  a  Takings  Clause  claim  for  dam-
age  that  was  not  prevented  by  an  attempt  at  military
protection  is  “when  the  military  had  reason  to  believe
that  its  action  placed  the  property  in  question  in  greater
peril  than  if  no  form  of  protection  had  been  provided  at
all.”  National  Board  of  Y.M.C.A.,  supra,  395  U.S.  at  95
(emphasis  in  original).
According  to  Justice  Harlan,
it  would  be  anamolous  to  allow  no  compensation  to  own-
ers  of  unprotected  property,  yet  allow  it  for  those  whose
property  was  inadequately  protected.
“It  is  only  if  the
military  or  other  protective  action  foreseeably  increased
the  risk  of  damage  that  compensation  should  be  re-
quired.”  Jd.  at  96  (emphasis  in  original).  By  the  same
token,  because  the  Fvurteenth  Amendment  imposes  no
duty  on  States  to  provide  any  protective  services  at  all,
it  would  be  equally  anamolous  to  predicate  liability  on
what  is  alleged  to  be  the  ineffective  provision  of  such
services.

These  basic  principles  of  due  process  analysis  are  fur-
ther  reinforced  by  the  “causation”  requirement  estab-
lished  under  Section  1983.  By  its  terms,  that  statute
applies  only  to  a  defendant  who  “subjects  or  causes  to
be  subjected”  any  citizen  to  a  constitutional  deprivation.
The  Court  has  made  clear  that  the  mere  failure  to  pre-
vent  action  by  another  party  is  insufficient  to  meet  this
requirement.  “[T]he  fact  that  Congress  did  specifically
provide  that  A’s  tort  became  B’s  liability  if  B  ‘caused’

18

19

(O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting).’*

A  to  subject  another  to  a  tort  suggests  that  Congress
did  not  intend  Section  1983  liability  to  attach  where
such  causation  was  absent.”  Monell,  supra,  436  U.S.  at
692.  See  also  City  of  Springfield  v.  Kibbe,  107  S.  Ct.
1114,  1119-21  (1987)
In-
deed,  even  when  it  enacted  Section  1986—the  provision
that  does  provide  a  cause  of  action  for  a  failure  to  pre-
vent  private  deprivations  of  civil  rights  *"—Congress
specifically  excluded  municipalities  from  the  purview  of
that  statute,  explaining  that  the  “Federal  Government
could  not  constitutionally  require  local  governments  to
create  police  forces,  whether  this  requirement  was  levied
directly,  or  indirectly  by  imposing  damages  for  breach
of  the  peace  on  municipalities.”  Monell,  supra,  436  U.S.
at  673."

12 The  debates  on  Section  1983  also  suggest  that  this  provision
was  intended  to  redress  only  active  infliction  and  not  State  inaction.
For  example,  Representative  Bingham,  the  author  of  Section  One  of
the  Fourteenth  Amendment,  stated  in  the  debates  on  Section  1983
that  “[t]he  States  never  had  the  right,  though  they  had  the
power,  to  inflict  wrongs  upon  free  citizens.”  Quoted  in  Monell,
supra,  436  U.S.  at  685  n.45.  He  then  chronicled  several  direct
governmental  deprivations  that  were  covered  by  the  statute,  none
of  which  were  remotely  similar  to  the  claim  in  this  case.  The
only  indication  of  liability  under  Section  1983  for  governmental
inaction  concerned  equal  protection  violations,  where  a  State  denies
services  for  discriminatory  reasons.  See  remarks  of  Rep.  Garfield,
quoted  ibid.  The  facts  of  this  case  implicate  no  such  invidious
discrimination.

13Tn  relevant  part,  Section  1986  provides  liability  for  “‘[e]very
person  who,  having  knowledge  that  [a  conspiracy  to  violate  civil
rights  is  afoot],  and  having  power  to  prevent  or  aid  in  preventing
the  Commission  of  the  same,  neglects  or  refuses  so  to  do...  .”  Be-
cause  Section  1986  specifically  incorporates  Section  1985,  it  applies
only  to  cases  involving  racial  or  similar  animus,  see  Griffen  v.
Breckenridge,  403  U.S.  88  (1971),  and  thus  has  no  conceivable  rele-
vance  to the  present  case.

14  The  legislative  debates  leading  to  the  rejection  of  the  so-called
“Sherman  Amendment,”  which  would  have  included  municipalities
within  the  reach  of  Section  1986,  is  reviewed  in  detail  in  Monell,

Adherence  to  an  “active  infliction”  requirement,  more-
over,  provides  the  essential  rationale  for  the  ruling  in
Monell  itself,  where  the  Court  held  that  iocal  govern-
ments  may  be  found  liable  under  Section  1983  but  not
on  a  respondeat  superior  basis.
If  mere  failure  to  pro-
tect  were  sufficient  to  impose  liability,  all  Section  1983
violations  perpetrated  by  municipal  officials  would  lead
to  municipal  liability  on  the  theory  that  the  government
failed  to  protect  the  individual  from  its  official’s  action.
Under  Monell,  however,  “it  is  when  execution  of  a  gov-
ernment’s  policy  or  custom  .
.  inflicts  the  injury  that
the  government  as  an  entity  is  responsible  under  Section
1983.”  Id.  at  694  (emphasis  supplied).  Whatever  de-
bates  have  raged  since  Monell  concerning  when  a  munici-
pality  has  so  acted,  see,  e.g.,  Praprotnick,  supra;  Tuttle,
supra;  City  of Canton  v.  Harris,  pending,  No.  86-1088,

lenged."*  Because  Section  1983  liability  must  be  pred-
a  municipality  cannot  be  held
icated  on  active  infliction,
liable  for  the  constitutional
torts  of  employees  over
whom  it  has  “the  mere  right  to  control  without  any  con-

proposition

essentially

central

.

The  Section  1983  requirement  that  a  Defendant  ac-
tively  inflict  harm  was  also  relied  on  in  Rizzo  v.  Goode,
423  U.S.  362  (1976),  where  the  Court  refused  to  find
constitutional  liability  premised  on  the  failure  of  a  city
police  department  to  act  in  the  face  of  an  alleged  pat-
tern  of  violations  by  individual  officers.  That  theory  was

supra,  436  U.S.  at  665-84.  That  discussion  also  fully  supports  the
conclusion  that  section  1983  was  not  intended  to  apply  to  municipal
failure  to  protect  against  the  private  infliction  of  harm.

1  But  see  Praprotnick,  supra,  108  S.  Ct.  at  924  n.1  (Stevens,  J.,
dissenting);  Pembaur  v.  City  of  Cincinnati,  475  U.S.  469,  489  n.4
(1986)  (Stevens,  J.,  concurring);  Tuttle,  supra,  471  U.S.  at  834-44
(Stevens,  J., dissenting).

20

deemed  insufficient  precisely  because  the  department  it-
self  did  not  actively  inflict  the  harm,  and  hence  it caused
no  constitutional  deprivation.
The  Court  thus  dis-
tinguished  Rizzo  from  Swann  v.  Charlotte-Mecklenburg
Bd.  of Educ.,  402  U.S.  1  (1971),  noting  that  in  the  latter
case,  “segregation  imposed  by  law  had  been  implemented
by state  authorities  for  varying  periods  of  time,  whereas
in  the  instant  case  the  District  Court  found  that  the
responsible  authorities  had  played  no  affirmative  part  in
depriving  any  member  of  the  two  respondent  classes  of
any  constitutional  rights.”  423  U.S.  at  377  (emphasis
supplied).
For  the  same  reasons,  Respondents  in  this
case  do  not  fall  on  the  liability  side  of  the  “affirmative”
deprivation  line  drawn  in  Rizzo,  supra.

violation

.

16  As  the  Court  stated  in  Rizzo:

The  theory  of  liability  underlying  the  District  Court’s  opinion,
and  urged  upon  us  by  respondents,  is  that  even  without  a
showing  of  direct  responsibility  for  the  actions  of  a  small  per-
centage  of  the  police  force,  petitioners’  failure  to  act  in  the
face  of a  statistical  pattern  is  indistinguishable  from  the  active
centage  of  the  police  force,  petitioners’  failure  to  act  in  the
conduct  enjoined  in  Hague  [v.  ClO,  307  U.S.  496  (1939)]  and
[Allee  v.]  Medrano  [, 416  U.S.  802  (1974)].  Respondents  posit
a  constitutional  “duty”  on  the  part  of  petitioners  (and  a  cor-
responding  “right”  of  the  citizens  of  Philadelphia)  to  “elimi-
nate”  future  police  misconduct;  a  “default”  of  that  affirmative
duty  being  shown  by  the  statistical  pattern,  the  District  Court
is  empowered  to  act  in  petitioners’  stead  and  take  whatever
preventive  measures  are  necessary,  within  its  discretion,  to
secure  the  “right”  at  issue.  Such  reasoning,  however,  blurs
accepted  usages  and  meanings  in  the  English  language  in  a  way
which  would  be  quite  inconsistent  with  the  words  Congress
chose  in  § 1983.  We  have  never  subscribed  to  these  amorphous
propositions  and  we  decline  to do so  now.
423  U.S.  at  375-76  (emphasis  in  original).

21

This  active  infliction  requirement—common  to  both
state  action  under  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  and  cau-
sation  under  Section  1983—is  especially  important  in
a  ease  such  as  this,  where  governmental  services  have
been  provided  but  are  argued  to  have  been  inadequate.
“It  would  be  ironic  that  when  a  State  embarks  on  such
a  desirable  experiment  it  thereby  opens  the  door  to
scrutiny  by  the  Federal  Courts,  while  States  that  choose
not  to  adopt  such  [laws]  entirely  avoid  the  strictures  of
the  due  process  clause.”  Hewitt  v.  Helms,  459  U.S.  460
(1983).  An  active  infliction  requirement  thus  serves  the
important  function  of  differentiating  between  the  provi-
sion  of  imperfect  services  and  the  abuse  of  government
power  with  the  intention  to  oppress,  only  the  latter
being  within  the  province  of  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment.  See  generally,  Daniels,  supra;  Davidson,  supra.”

17  The  active  infliction  requirement  has  generally  been  followed
by  all  but  one  of  the  Circuits,  although  two  others  in  dicta  have
allowed  for  the  possibility  of  broader  liability.  The  District  of
Columbia,  First,  Second,  Fifth,  Sixth,  Seventh,  Eighth  and  Eleventh
Circuits  have  all  found  that  the  State  is  constitutionally  obligated
to  provide  protection  only  when  the  State  has  actively  inflicted
harm  by  putting  that  individual  in  a  worse  position  than  he  would
have  been  in  had  the  State  not  been  involved.  See  Washington  v.
District  of Columbia,  802  F.2d  1478,  1481  (D.C.  Cir.  1986);  Estate
of Gilmore  v.  Buckley,  787  F.2d  714,  721  (1st  Cir.)  cert.  denied,  107
S.Ct.  270  (1986);  McClary  v.  O'Hare,  786  F.2d  83,  88-89  (2nd  Cir.
1986)  : Doe  v.  New  York  Dep’t  of Social  Services,  649  F.2d  134,  141
(2d  Cir.  1981),  cert.  denied,  464  U.S.  864  (1983):  Rankin  v.  City  of
Wichita  Falls,  762  F.2d  444,  449  (5th  Cir.  1985);  Nishiyama  v.
Dickson  County,  814  F.2d  277  (6th  Cir.  1987);  Ellsworth  v.  City  of
Racine,  774  F.2d  182,  185  (7th  Cir.  1985),  cert.  denied,  475  U.S.
1047  (1986):  Archie  v.  City  of  Racine,  847  F.2d  1211  (7th  Cir.
1988)  (en  banc):  Jackson  v.  Byrne,  738  F.2d  1443,  1446  (7th  Cir.
1984):  Jackson  v.  City  of  Joliet,  715  F.2d  1200,  1203-04  (7th  Cir.
1983),  cert.  denied,  465  U.S.  1049  (1984);  Harpole  v.  Arkansas
Dep’t  of Human  Services,  820  F.2d  923  (8th  Cir.  1987);  Bradberry
v.  Pinellas  County,  789  F.2d  1513,  1517  (11th  Cir.  1986).

Somewhat  less  settled  is  the  rule  in  the  Fourth  and  Ninth  Cir-
cuits,  whose  holdings  comport  with  this  test  but,  under  the  aegis
of  “special  relationship,”  allow  in  dicta  that  “a  right  to  protection

Bailey  may  have  limited  even  that  holding  when  it  was  later  dis-

F.2d  12,  16  (3rd  Cir.  1987).

or  neglect  may  request  an  immediate  investigation  by  the  Sheriff

See  48.09.  And  it  was  Assistant  Corporate  Coun-
