INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  IN  THE  CAUSE
BEFORE  THE  COURT

The  Massachusetts  Committee  For

Children  and  Youth  ("MCCY")  is  a

non-profit  organization,  headquartered

in  Boston,  which  has  worked  for  three

decades,  through  advocacy,  to  improve

the  lives  of  Massachusetts’  most

vulnerable  children,  including  abused

and  neglected  children,  foster

children,  drug-addicted  newborns,

adolescents  facing  depression  and

suicide,  abandoned  and  runaway

children,  and  homeless  children  and

their  families.

The  President  of  MCCY,  Dr.  Eli

Newberger,  is  the  chief  of  the  clinic

at  Boston's  Children's  Hospital

responsible  for  treatment  of  cases  of

abused  and  neglected  children.

Dr.

Newberger  has  written  numerous  books

and  articles  on  the  field  of  child

abuse.

Members  of  MCCY's  Board  include

child  advocates  and  professionals  in

the  child  welfare  field.
issued  numerous  studies  in  the  fields
of  child  welfare,  including  reports  on

MCCY  has

child  abuse  prevention,  adolescent

services,  and  services  for  homeless

children.

MCCY  submits  this  amicus  curiae

brief  because  it  believes  that  a

correct  interpretation  of  42  U.S.C.

§1983,  as  asserted  by  the  plaintiff,
will  be  in  the  public  interest  and  will

benefit  abused  children.

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT

In  this  brief,  MCCY  contends  that:

1.

The  intervention  in  Joshua's

case  by  the  Department  and  Kemmeter

failed  to  meet  fundamental  and  clearly

articulated  standards  of  practice.

Under  those  standards,  the  facts  of  the

plaintiff's  case  support  a  finding  of

deliberate  indifference  toward  Joshua.
The  intervention  in  Joshua's

2.

family  life  by  Kemmeter  and  the

Department  profoundly  affected  the
dynamics  of  the  family  and,  more  likely
than  not,  increased  the  risk  to  Joshua.

3.

Under  well-established

Fourteenth  Amendment  precedent  the

state  had  a  duty  to  protect  the  life

and  bodily  integrity  interest  of  the

plaintiff.

4.

Denial  of  a  claim  under  §1983

will  have  the  result  of  leaving  Joshua

without  an  adequate  remedy.

5.

The  class  of  persons  to  whom

§1983  protection  should  be  accorded

under  the  applicable  “special
relationship"  test  is  narrow,  and  their

need  for  protection  is  great.

6.

Recognition  of  the  plaintiff's

right  to  proceed  under  §1983  will  not
Rather,
distort  social  work  practice.
it  will  create  an  appropriate  deterrent
to  indifference  to  danger  to  vulnerable

children.

7.

Given  the  nature  of  the  legal

and  factual  issues  at  stake,  the
federal  courts  are  the  only  appropriate
forum  for  this  suit  to  redress  4
violation  of  constitutional  rights.

ARGUMENT

The  Case  Below

This  case  arose  from  life-
threatening  injuries  inflicted  upon
four-year-old  Joshua  DeShaney
("Joshua")  by  his  father  Randy  DeShaney
("DeShaney"),  and  from  the  failure  of
the  Winnebago  Department  of  Social
Services  (the  “Department” ) and  social
worker  Ann  Kemmeter  ("Kemmeter"™)  to

make  good  faith  efforts  to  protect

Joshua  against  such  harm.

Joshua

suffered  severe  brain  damage  as  a

result.

He  most  likely  will  be

institutionalized  for  life.

Joshua,  by  his  guardian  ad  litem,

and  his  mother,  Melody  DeShaney,

brought  suit  against  Kemmeter,  the

Department  and  Winnebago  County.

The

suit  charged  the  defendants  with  having

deprived  Joshua  of  his  liberty  without

due  process  of  law,  in  violation  of  42

U.S.C.  §1983.

The  United  States

District  Court  of  Wisconsin  dismissed

the  action  on  summary  judgment.

The

Seventh  Circuit  in  DeShaney  v.

Winnebago  County  Department  of  Social

Services,  812  F.2d  298  (1987),  affirmed

the  dismissal.

MCCY  contends  that  the  Circuit

Court's  opinion  was  based  on  an

erroneous  view  of  the  facts  about  child
abuse  cases  in  general  and  this  case  in
particular  and  represents  4  misapplica-
tion  of  law  under  §1983  and  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.

The  plaintiff

As  the

has  a  cognizable  claim.
underlying  facts  are  in  dispute,  the
grant  of  summary  judgment  was  in  error.
Structure  of  the  Argument
The  principal  issue  before  the
Court  is  the  existence  of  a  Fourteenth
Amendment  duty  of  the  agents  of  the
state  to  act  in  good  faith  to  protect

MCCY  will  discuss  the

Joshua  DeShaney.
relevant  facts  of  this  case  and  of
child  abuse  in  general  and  demonstrate
that  in  light  of  these  factors,  as
discussed  herein,  4  Fourteenth
Amendment’  duty  must  exist.

Furthermore,  MCCY  believes  that

the  basic  standards  of  social  work

practice  for  child  abuse  cases  are

appropriate  for  litigation  in  federal

court,  with  the  aid  of  expert

testimony.

MCCY  will  demonstrate,  in

the  context  the  facts  of  the  case,

there  was  a  gross  and  obvious  failure

to  follow  basic  criteria  of  child  abuse

investigation  and  intervention.

The  Pattern  of  Child  Abuse

Although  physical  and  sexual  abuse

of  children  surely  dates  back  to  pre-

historic  times,  it  is  only  in  recent

times  that  our  culture  has  recognized

child  abuse  as  endemic  in  all  human

societies.

The  pattern  of  behavior  of  parents

that  leads  to  child  abuse  has  become

quite  well  understood.

A  review  of  the  literature
relating  to  the  emotional
and  personality  character-
istics  of  child  abusers
reveals  a  recurring
pattern.

Child  abusers

are  often  described  as
immature,  self-centered,
emotionally  starved,  and
having  unmet  dependence
needs...  (M]Jany  abusers
feel  inadequate  or
incompetent  in  their
particular  role.

Most  child  abuse  incidents
occur  under  conditions  of
accumulated  stress  in
which  the  abuser  feels
overwhelmed  and  unable  to
cope  with  his  or  her
problems...  The  abuser  out
of  frustration  explodes
with  violence  which  is
directed  toward  a  child  in
a  futile  attempt  to  cope
with  his  or  her  stressful
situation.
target  under  these
circumstances.
omitted]...

Any  child  is  a

for  the  Child  Abuse  and  Neglect  Service

JOURNAL  333,  334  (1977).

It  is  hard  to  imagine  any  human
condition  more  helpless  than  that  of
Children  who
the  abused  young  child.

are  the  subject  of  abuse  are  totally

isolated.

They  live  in  a  world  of

terror  and  pain.

Often  child  abusers

successfully  intimidate  the  children

into  refraining  from  alerting  outside

authorities  to  their  plight,  even  when

the  children  are  old  enough  to  do  so.

See,  e.g.,  Doe  v.  New  York  City

Department  of  Social  Services,  649  F.2d

134,  138  (2d  Cir.  1981).

Toddlers,  such  as  four-year-old

Joshua,  are  especially  vulnerable

because  they  rarely  have  contact  with

persons  or  institutions  outside  the

family,  except  as  permitted  by  their

parents.

They  obviously  cannot  protect

themselves,  and  they  cannot  escape  from

their  violent  caretakers.

Physical  abuse  has  a  profoundly

adverse  effect  on  the  child's  ability

human  being.

Even  infants  and  toddlers

are  affected  by  serious  abuse,  and  |
become  conditioned  to  acceptance  of

their  mistreatment.

[An]  outstanding  charac-
teristic  of  abused  infants
(and  of  older  children  too)
is  their  passive  compliance
and  acceptance  of  whatever
happens  to  them  or  is
They
expected  of  them;  ...
appear  very  stoic,  listless,
apathetic  children  who
display  little  affect.
seems  that  some  children
learn  very  young  that  their
best  hope  of  getting  food  and
care  in  a  hostile  or  chaotic
environment  where  parents  are
preoccupied  with  their  own
needs  seems  to  be  total
submission  to  their  parents’
wishes.

The  State's  Actions  in  Joshua's
Case  Failed  to  Meet  Basic  Norms
of  Case  Practice

In  the  case  below,  the  Seventh

Circuit  concluded  that  the  Department

and  Kemmeter  were  not  responsible  for

Joshua's  welfare,  and  did  not

contribute  to  Joshua's  peril.

As  will

be  shown  herein,  that  premise  is

incorrect.

Moreover,  the  Circuit  Court

implied  that  the  standards  governing

protective  service  cases  are  somehow

unfit  for  litigation  in  a  federal

court.

In  fact,  the  standards  are

clear,  and  can  be  proven  to  the  trier

of  fact  by  expert  testimony  admitted

under  Fed.  R.  Evid.  701  through  705,

just  as  in  other  cases.

Basic  criteria  and  practices  for

recognizing  and  responding  to  child

abuse  are  the  core  of  the  training  of  a

protective  service  worker.

These  clear

standards  were  ignored  in  Joshua

DeShaney's  case.

Indeed,  to  one

familiar  with  abusing  families  and

children,  the  case  of  Joshua  DeShaney

showed  many  red  flags  of  serious  abuse

and  of  great  danger  to  the  child.

Kemmeter's  own  case  record  shows  her

awareness  of  the  indicators  of  abuse

and  the  fact  that  Josrua  was  being

battered  repeatedly.

Yet  Kemmeter  did

not  take  any  good  faith  steps  to  help

and  protect  Joshua.

-  14 -

‘.

Failure  to  Seek  Medical
Diagnosis  of  Joshua's
Head  Injuries

The  most  important  rule  of

practice  which  Kemmeter  violated  in

Joshua's  case  is  the  need  for  medical

diagnosis  of  repeated  serious  traumatic

injury.

Medical  diagnosis  is  needed

both  for  a  reliable  analysis  and  for

evidence  for  court.

Such  diagnosis

should  be  conducted  with  the  consent  of

the  parents  if  possible,  or  under  court

order  if  necessary.

As  every  protective  services

social  worker  knows,  head  injuries  are

the  most  dangerous  result  of  child

battering,  and  the  most  frequent

medical  cause  of  child  abuse

deaths.

In  children,  “[{a]J1ll  head

injuries  have  to  be  considered  as

potentially  very  serious  because  of

possible  damage  to  the  brain  and

central  nervous  system".

The  Responsibility  of  the  State
For  The  Plight  of  the  Child

It  is  important  to  note  that  in  a

child  protection  system,  the  social

worker  (and  his  or  her  superviso: 3)

preempt  society's  response  to  the

abusive  family.

As  both  a  legal  and  a

practical  matter,  only  the  protective

service  agency  can  direct  services  to

the  abused  child  and  the  family.

Other

institutions  who  might  intervene  to

protect  a  child,  such  as  the  police,

hospitals,  social  service  agencies,  the

child's  school  and  church,  are  all

inhibited  from  acting,  because  all

believe  that  the  social  service  agency

is  in  charge.

Because  the  protective  service

agency  has  preempted  the  field,  it,  in

fact,  increases  the  child's  isolation,

-  26 -

-  27 -

and  thereby  increases  the  danger  to  the

chiid  when  its  intervention  is  carried

child.

Protective  service  interven-

tions  profoundly  affect  the  dynamics  of

out  in  an  inept  and  indifferent  manner.

the  family.

In  November  1983,  Joshua  was

hospitalized  for  a  second  time  for  a

cut  forehead,  bloody  nose,  swollen  ear,

and  bruises  on  both  shoulders.

The

hospital  could  have  sought  help  from  a

private  agency  or  gone  to  the  Court  to

seek  protection  for  Joshua,  or  they

could  have  contacted  the  police.

Plainly  the  emergency  room  personnel

did  none  of  these  things,  because  they

the  family's  problems
rather  than  alleviate  them.

support  for  the  family  through  frequent

visits  and  services.

-  31  -

Under  the  principles  set  forth  in

this  case  law,  if  the  administrator  of

a  mental  hospital,  by  deliberate

indifference,  allowed  a  patient  known

to  be  violent  to  kill  or  maim  other

patients,  the  victims  would  surely  have

a  due  process  claim.

Practically

speaking,  there  is  no  difference

between  that  situation  and  a  case  in

which  a  social  worker,  by  deliberate

indifference,  allows  a  parent  to  kill

Or  maim  a  child.

In  each  case  the

victim  is  totally  helpless,  and  the

only  possible  source  of  aid  is  the

state.

In  each  case,  the  state  has

intentionally  assumed  the  role  of  the

protector  of  the  victim.

In  each  case,

the  indifference  of  the  state  to  likely

injury  and  death  of  a  helpless  victim

is  offensive  to  the  fundamental  values

of  our  culture.

While  the  Constitution

does  not  require  that  mental  hospitals

or  protective  services  for  abused

children  be  provided,  once  they  are

provided,  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  sets

minimal  standards  of  civilized  conduct

for  state  action  in  both  settings.

Abused  children  represent  the

absolute  extreme  case  of  a  vulnerable

group.

Because  the  Fourteenth

Amendment  life  and  bodily  integrity

interests  of  abused  children  are

completely  dependent  on  the  state,  the

federal  courts  should  provide  at  least

a  minimal  level  of  judicial  scrutiny  of

the  acts  of  the  state  after  it

recognizes  their  danger  and  becomes

involved  in  their  lives.

The  Court  Should  Adopt  An
Appropriately  Narrow  Version
of  the  Special  Relationship  Test

Several  federal  courts  have

allowed  recovery  in  cases  similar  to

-  32 -

=» 33 -

the  case  at  bar  on  the  basis  of  the

“special  relationship"  doctrine.

In

Estate  of  Bailey  by  Oare  v.  County  of

York,  768  F.2d  503  (3d  Cir.  1985),  the

court  found  that  a  duty  of

constitutional  dimension  was  created

when  a  state  protective  services  agency

learned  of  “special  danger"  to  the

child.

Id.  at  510-511.

A  similar  case

is  Jensen  v.  Conrad,  747  F.2d  185  (4th

Cir.  1984).

In  that  case,  the  court

embraced  the  “special  relationship"

analysis,  albeit  in  dictum. ’  The

court  wrote:

“a  right  to  affirmative

protection  need  not  be  limited  by  a

determination  that  there  was  a

‘custodial  relationship'."

747  F.2d  at

194.

_

7  The  court  affirmed  a

dismissal  of  the  action  on  the  grounds
of  qualified  immunity.
194-196.

Id.  at
ary,

-  34 -

MCCY  believes  that  the  test  for  a

“special  relationship”  should  be

limited  to  those  who  are  in  genuine

need  of  special  protection,  such  as

children  and  other  incompetents  who  are

known  to  be  at  serious  risk  of  physical

harm.

The  focus  should  be  on  the

following  factors:

(a)  danger  to  the

victim;  (b)  the  helplessness  of  the

victim;  (c)  knowledge  by  the  state  of

the  threat  to  the  victim;  and  (d)  the

assumption  of  the  role  of  protector  by

the  state.

This  narrowed  approach  will

provide  protection  to  those  who  need  it

most  and  will  avoid  undue  expansion  of

§1983  lawsuits.

MCCY  urges  the  Court  to  reject  a

formalistic  approach  in  which  liability

turns  on  whether  or  not  the  state  has

legal  custody  of  the  child-plaintiff.

Whether  or  not  the  state  obtains  legal

-  35 -

custody  of  a  child  is  often  a  matter  of

happenstance,  with  little  relation  to

the  risk  to  the  child.

The  state  may

have  legal  custody  of  an  abandoned

child  placed  with  his  or  her

Grandparents,  in  a  situation  where

there  is  no  cause  for  special  scrutiny

of  the  home.

Or  the  state  may,  as  in

Joshua's  case,  have  assumed

life-and-death  responsibility  for,  but

not  custody  of,  a  child  receiving

dangerous  and  repeated  injury  from  his

Own  parents.

The  Court  should  adopt  a

pragmatic  approach  focusing  on  whether

the  state  has  assumed  responsibility

for  a  child  whom  it  knows  is  vulnerable

and  at  risk.

Abused  Children  Have  No  Effective
Remedy,  Other  Than  A  Lawsuit

The  remedy  provided  under

Wisconsin  tort  law  is  inadequate  to

provide  the  relief  necessary  for  Joshua

DeShaney.

Under  Wisconsin  law,  the

amount  recoverable  for  claims  against

governmental  bodies,  officers  and

employees  in  any  action  founded  on  tort

may  not  exceed  $50,000.

Wis.  Stat.

§893.80(3),

No  punitive  damages  are

allowed.

Id.

$50,000  is  woefully

inadequate  to  provide  relief  for  a

child  who  has  suffered  permanent  brain

damage  and  who  is  confined  for  life  to

an  institution  for  the  retarded.

A  §1983  remedy  is  necessary  as

well,  because  the  state  protective

services  systems  in  America  are  largely

immune  from  oversight.

Federal  law

requires  that  every  child's  case  be

confidential.

42  U.S.C.

§5103(b)(2)(E).

Wisconsin  law  provides

the  same.

Wis.  Stat.  §48.981(7).

Cases  of  gross  failure  are  often  buried

in  the  agency's  files.

As  every  child

-  36  -

oe 97 «

advocate  knows,  litigation  has  the

power  to  throw  light  on  social

institutions  affecting  children  that

fail  to  fulfill  their  mission.

An  inquiry  of  the  Department's

actions  and  policies,  through  the

vehicle  of  this  litigation,  may  be  the

only  chance  that  abused  children  in

Wisconsin  may  have  for  redress  of

systemic  flaws  of  the  child  protection

system.

If  that  avenue  of  reform  is

closed,  children  will  suffer  needlessly.

The  Cause  of  Action  Will  Net
Unduly  Interfere  With  Social  Work
Case  Practice

The  Circuit  Court  argued  that

social  workers  will  be  placed  on  a

“razor's  edge,  where  if  it  terminates

parental  rights  it  is  exposed  to  a

section  1983  suit  ...  by  the  parent  and

if  it  fails  to  terminate  those  rights

it  is  exposed  to  a  section  1983  suit  by

the  child."

812  F.2d  at  304.

The

assertion  of  this  alleged  dichotomy  is

wrong.

Under  well-established  principles,

a  worker,  or  the  state,  will  only  have

§1983  liability  when  there  has  been

“deliberate  indifference”  to  the  fate

of  the  child.

See  Estelle  v.  Gamble,

429  U.S.  97,  106  (1976)  (the  Supreme

Court  applied  the  “deliberate

indifference"  standard  in  connection

with  a  violation  of  the  Eighth

Amendment).

See  also  Davidson  v.

Cannon,  474  U.S.  344,  347  (1986)  (the

Court  held  that  a  showing  of  mere

negligence  was  insufficient  to  invoke

compensation  under  §1983).

Good  faith

error  or  simple  negligence  wili  not,

and  should  not,  lead  to  liability.

Only  very  clear  cases  of  violation  of

the  basic  rules  of  social  work  practice

-  38 -

—

in  cases  of  manifest  danger  to  children

will  survive  a  motion  for  summary

judgment.

Rather  than  a  “razor's  edge",

there  is  in  fact  a  broad  range  of  good

faith  protection  for  social  workers  and

protective  services  agencies.

Only  in

extreme  cases,  as,  for  example,  when

the  agent  of  the  state  recklessly

removes  a  child  from  its  parents,  or

when  by  deliberate  indifference  it

allows  a  child  to  be  seriously  harmed

or  killed,  will  there  be  liability.

This  is  plainly  the  appropriate

standard.

MCCY  does  not  believe  that  this

Standard  will  distort  social  work

practice  or  lead  to  automatic  removal

of  childr>:  from  their  home  in  every

case  of  reported  abuse.

Good  social

workers  will  continue  to  do  good  social

work.

Rather,  the  §1983  action  will

serve  as  a  deterrent  to  deliberate

indifference  to  the  danger  to  abused

children,  and  will  thereby  provide

protection  for  the  nation's  children.

The  Federal  Courts  Are  The
Appropriate  Forum  For  This  Case

All  of  the  criteria  and  standards

of  social  work  practice  discussed  above

are  suitable  for  expert  testimony  --

testimony  which  would  be  quite  simple,

clear  and  direct.

The  suggestion  of

the  C.rcuit  Court  that  state  courts  are

somehow  specially  competent  to  deal

with  these  cases  such  as  these  is

unfounded.

812  F.2d  at  304.

If

anything,  the  reverse  is  true.

Federal

courts  have  played  a  significant  role

in  children's  rights  litigation.

E.g.,

Lynch  v.  Dukakis,  719  F.2d  504  (lst

Cir.  1983);  Doe  v.  New  York  City  Dept.

of  Social  Services,  709  F.2d  782  (2d

-  40 -

-  4] -

Cir.  1983);  Doe  v.  Klein,  599  F.2d  338

(9th  Cir.  1979);  Youakim  v.  Miller,  562

F.2d  483  (7th  Cir.  1977).

Moreover,  as

is  discussed  above,  child  protection  is

The  grant  of  summary  judgment,

affirmed  by  the  Circuit  Court,  should

be  reversed,  and  the  case  should  be

allowed  to  proceed  to  a  trial  on  the

regulated  by  federal  law.

And  there  is

merits.

obviously  no  need  for  the  trial  court

to  have  its  own  "staff  of  social

workers"  (as  the  Circuit  Court  suggests

at  812  F.2d  at  304)  to  try  a  case  such

as  this  one.

Moreover  because  cases

like  this  one  involve  the  protection  of

federal  rights,  a  federal  forum  is

obviously  appropriate.

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  the

decision  below  should  be  reversed,  and

the  case  should  be  allowed  to  proceed

to  trial.

Respectfully  submitted,

Laura  L.  Carroll

boards

and

their

97,000

members.

educational

III.  THE  DUE  PROCESS  CLAUSE  PROTECTION  OF
"LIBERTY  INTERESTS"  EXTENDS  ONLY  TO
PROCEDURAL  CONCERNS.

Although  Petitioners  cannot  show  a

Substantive  due  process  right  which  could

arguably  be  said  to  be  abridged  in  this

case,

it

should

be

determined

whether

there

is

a

liberty

interest

meriting

protection  for  purposes  of  procedural  due

process.

And,  if  so,  whether  the  state

"deprives"  a  person  of  the  interest,  under

42  U.S.C.  Section  1983,  by  action  which  is

not  intentional.

A  number  of  “liberty”  interests  which

are  protected  by  the  procedural  guarantees

of  the  fourteenth  amendment,  even  though

they  may  not  be  recognized  as  “fundamental

rights."

"These  interests  attain  this

constitutional  status  by  virtue  of  the

fact

that

they

have

been

initially

oS  «

recognized  and  protected  by  state  law...."

Paul  _v.  Davis,  424  U.S.  707,  711  (1976).

By  establishing

a

social

services

agency

with

a

mission

of

protecting

children,  the  state  does  not  necessarily

confer

a

constitutional

right

to

the

service.

If  that  were  true,  every  public

agency,  including  school  districts,  police

and  fire  departments  and  hospitals  would

be  subject  to  a  duty  to  provide  services

to  all;

a  loss  which  could

fairly  be

attributed

to

the

state's

failure

to

provide  the  service,  would  be  actionable

under  the  due  process  clause.

The  state  must  do  more  than  merely

offer  a  service  before  a  service  recipient

can  claim  a  right  to  the  service.

In

Board  of  Regents  v.  Roth,  408  U.S.  564

(1972),

this

Court

held

that

a

state

statute  could  give  rise  to  a  property  or

liberty  interest

that  merited  procedural

—

due  processs  protection,  but  in  order  for

such  an  entitlement  to  be  present,  the

Court

said

it  was

not  enough  for  one

to

have  an  abstract  need

or  desire  for  a

benefit,

but

instead

one

must

have

a

“legitimate  claim  of  entitlement"  to  the

benefit.

In  subsequent  cases,  this  Court

has  framed  the  entitlement  issue  in  terms

of  whether  the  statute  places

any

“real

limits

on

decisionmaker

discretion.”

Board  of  Pardons  v.  Allen,  107  S.Ct.  2415,

2423  (1987)  (O'Connor,  J.  dissenting)

also  Olim  v.  Wakenekona,  461  U.S.
(1983);  Hewitt  v.  Helms,  459  U.S.

of

from

And,  there  is  no  policy  or

statute  which  gave  rise  to  the  injury.

due  process  violation  for  two  reasons.

because

there

interest"  created  either  by  state  law  or

from

its

even

con

derations

and

courts

protect

against  deliberate  abuse  of  power,  not

ineptitude.

No.  87-154

IN  THE

SUPREME  COURT  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES

OCTOBER  TERM,  1987

JOSHUA  DESHANEY,  a  Minor,  by his  Guardian  ad
Litem,  and  Melody  DeShaney,

Petitioners,

Vv.

WINNEBAGO  COUNTY  DEPARTMENT  OF  SOCIAL  SERVICES;
WINNEBAGO  COUNTY;  ANN  KEMMETER  AND  CHERYL
STELSE,

Respondents.

Brief  of the  States  of  New  York,  Connecticut,
Maryland,  Oregon,  Pennsylvania  and
Wisconsin  as  Amici  Curiae  in  Support  of  Respondents

Interest  of Amici  Curiae

Amicus  State  of  New  York  and  the  other  amici  States
respectfully  submit  this  brief  pursuant  to  Supreme  Court
Rule  36.4  in  support  of respondents.  The  amici  urge  this
Court  to  affirm  the  judgment  of the  court  below.

The  amicus  State  of  New  York  indirectly  funds  a signif-
icant  portion  of the  child  protective  caseworker  activity  in

2

3

New  York  State.  While  many  of  these  caseworkers  are
county  employees,  State  revenue  sharing  assists  in  the
provision  of  these  services.  Additionally,  the  State,
through  its  Department  of Social  Services,  provides  train-
ing and  continuing  education  for  caseworkers.  The  State
plays  an  indirect  role  in  the  quality  of  care  furnished  by
the  caseworkers  by  distribution  of  a  Child  Protective
Services  Program  Manual.  This  Manual,  which  reprints
relevant  statutes,  regulations  and  forms  for  court  pro-
ceedings,  also  enumerates  guidelines  and  procedures  for
child  protective  caseworkers.  The  Manual  suggests
methods  of investigating  and  documenting  suspected  inci-
dents  of  child  abuse  and  maltreatiment  so  that  available
evidence  can  be  presented  in  usable  form  to  prosecutors
and  courts.  The  State  Department  of Social  Services  also
maintains  a  computerized,  centralized  register  of  child
abuse  allegations  and  provides  this  information  to  local
caseworkers  and  courts.  In  sum,  the  State  is significantly
involved  in  overseeing  the  provision  of child  care  protec-
tive  services,  even  though  local  government  employees
generally  are  responsible  for  actual  delivery  of these  serv-
ices  in  the  field.

While  the  details  of  the  programs  and  statutes  vary
from  state  to  state,  the  description  of  New  York’s  child
care  protective  services  system  is generally  similar  to  those
in  the  other  amici  states.

Thus,  amici  have  a  financial,  a  professional  and  an  op-
erational  interest  in  this  case  which  involves  potential
“constitutional  tort’’  liability  of  child  protective  case-
workers  and  their  supervisors.  Therefore,  this  brief  ex-
presses  the  concerns  of  amici’s  child  protective  case-
workers,  as  well  as  the  interests  of state  and  local  govern-
ment  agencies.

The  amici  submit  that  child  protective  officials,  such  as
caseworkers  and  their  supervisors,  should  not  be exposed
to  money  damages  liability  under  42 USC  § 1983  (and,  de-
rivatively,  to attorneys’  fee awards  under  42 USC  § 1988),
when,  as  here,  the  caseworkers  do  not  themselves  have
physical  custody  of a  child  subsequently  abused  by a par-
ent  in  lawful  custody  of  the  child.  Regardless  of  how
characterized,  omissions  of child  protective  caseworkers
to  take  action  to  upset  the  strong  presumption  of parental
custody  of  children  should  not  be  actionable  under  42
USC  § 1983  as  a violation  of the  due  process  clause  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment.

The  amici  recognize  the  unfortunate  tragedy  that  was
inflicted  by an  abusive  parent  upon  the  infant  petitioner
in  this  case.  Yet,  in  this  and  similar  cases,  governmental
providers  of  supportive  social  services  are  limited—by
constitutional  constraints,  by  statutory  provisions,  by
court  decisions  on  burdens  of proof  as  to  custody  of chil-
dren  and  termination  of  parental  rights,  by  heavy  case-
loads,  and  by  availability  of  staff  and  resources—from
taking  action  in  every  case  which,  in  hindsight,  might
have  prevented  injury  if parental  rights  were  challenged,
contested  or  deprived.  More  importantly,  child  protective
caseworkers,  despite  the  best  of  motivation  and  training,
are  unable  reliably  to  predict  whether  parents  will  abuse
children.

Therefore,  amici  respectfully  submit  this  brief  to  artic-
ulate  the  problems  and  concerns  of  the  child  protective
caseworkers  and  supervisors  whose  ability  to  discharge
their  professional  responsibilities  is  threatened  by  peti-
tioners’  lawsuit  now  before  this  Court.

4

Summary  of  Argument

While  the  court  below  properly  affirmed  the  dismissal
of the  complaint,  it did  so  on  the  basis  that  the  due  proc-
ess  clause  does  not  furnish  a  ‘‘constitutional  tort’’  remedy
against  non-custodial  governmental  actors,  even  for  er-
rors  or  omissions  claimed  to  involve  gross  negligence,  de-
liberate  indifference,  or  willful  and  wanton  misconduct.
We  agree  with  that  result,  but  we  first  discuss  another
sound  and  simple  reason  why  liability  could  not  exist  in
this  case.  In  our  view,  regardless  of  whether  petitioner’s
allegations  might  otherwise  state  a  cause  of  action,  they
are  nonetheless  an  insufficient  predicate  for  liability,  as
absolute  immunity  should  insulate  child  protective  case-
workers  and  their  supervisors  from  liability.

In  many  respects,  the  job  of  a  child  protective  case-
worker  resembles  that  of  a  prosecutor  or  parole  officer.
Each  performs  a  quasi-judicial  task  of  measuring  facts
and  circumstances  against  a  set  of  general  criteria  (e.g.,
constitutional,  statutory  and  regulatory).  Each  must  de-
cide  whether  evidence,  often  circumstantial,  is  sufficient
to  justify  formal  charges.  Each  must  be  mindful  of  the
burdens  of  proof  the  courts  interpose.  Each  must  temper
judgmental,  discretionary  decisions  with  the  practical
consequences  that  time  and  effort  devoted  to  presenting
or  prosecuting  unsustainable  charges  will  divert  resources
from  other  cases  and  other  responsibilities.  Yet,  because
none  of  these  officers  are  able  rel.»bly  to  predict  future
human  behavior,  none  of them  are  or  should  be subject  to
federally  imposed  liability  for  the  performance  of  their
quasi-judicial  tasks.  All  the  more  so  should  child  protec-
tive  caseworkers  not  be  exposed  to  damages  liability  un-
der  42  USC  § 1983  because  of the  overwhelming  constitu-
tional,  statutory  and  regulatory  presumptions  in  favor  of
parental  custody  and  decisionmaking  authority.

5

Moreover,  a  rule  of  liability  for  social  services  case-
workers  would  place  them  in  the  untenable  situation  of
being  sued  for  either  doing  too  much  or  too  little;  in  the
former  case  by  parents  deprived  of  custody  even  tempo-
rarily,  under  an  aggressive  policy  of  initiating  child  pro-
tective  proceedings;  in the  latter  case  by children  like  peti-
tioner.

Alternatively,  and  apart  from  questions  of  quasi-
judicial  absolute  immunity,  the  failure  adequately  to  fur-
nish  child  protective  services  in  a  non-custodial  context,
simply  is  not  actionable  under  the  due  process  clause.
Cases  involving  direct  institutional  custody,  such  as  men-
tal  health  facilities  and/or  prisons,  are  readily  distinguish-
able.  A  non-custodial  situation,  with  periodic  visits  or  in-
vestigations  of  allegations  of  child  abuse,  simply  lacks
both  the  actual  control,  as  well  as  the  theoretical  oppor-
tunity  for  control,  which  is central  to  the  finding  of  lia-
bility  in institutional  settings  of prisons  or  mental  hygiene
facilities.

More  generally,  as  the  court  below  recognized,  govern-
mental  services  which  seek  to  monitor,  supervise  or  regu-
late  conduct  of  third  parties  cannot  guarantee  safety.
Government  may  assist  in  the  enhancement  of  relative
safety  of others,  but  the  due  process  clause  does  not  fur-
nish  a  constitutional  guarantee  of  safety  from  harm.
There  is no  element  of governmental  “abuse  of power”  in
this  type  of case,  which,  at  most,  involves  a  failure  to  fur-
nish  more  or  better  protection  from  harm  inflicted  by
third  parties.

There  are  also  other  institutional  concerns  which  coun-
sel  restraint  before  a  broad  avenue  of new  liability  should
be  recognized.  It will  almost  always  be  possible  to  state  a
cause  of  action  on  the  pleadings  if gross  negligence  or
recklessness  were  deemed  actionable,  because  almost  any

6

>

error  Or  Omission  can  be  characterized  in  those  terms.  It
will  almost  always  be  possible  to  allege  that  the  govern-
ment,  by its inaction,  failed  to  prevent  a harm  perpetrated
by  a  third  party.  Government,  which  merely  provides
services,  will  be set  up as  a guarantor  of safety.  The  feder-
alization  of  tort  law,  even  in  the  present  context,  will  in-
vite  a  flood  of new  federal  lawsuits  across  the  spectrum  of
garden-variety  personal  injury  actions.  Moreover,  the
prospect  of  an  award  of  attorneys’  fees  pursuant  to  42
USC  § 1988  offers  a  strong  incentive  to  litigate  these
claims  in  federal  court.

In  short,  the  consequences  of  federalizing  tort  law  are
profound  indeed.  The  sympathetic  facts  of this  case  can-
not  be  denied,  but  sympathy  should  not  substitute  for
law,  especially  when,  as  here,  to  do so  would  be to  open  a
Pandora’s  Box  of liability.

The  judgment  of  the  court  below  should  be  affirmed.

ARGUMENT

POINT  I

This  case  may  be  decided,  and  the  judgment  below  af-
firmed,  on  the  narrow  ground  that  social  services  case-
workers  (and  their  supervisors)  perform  classic  quasi-ju-
dicial  tasks,  and  hence,  are  protected  by  absolute
immunity  from  money  damages  liability.

While  we  believe  the  court  below  properly  decided  that
allegations  of  gross  negligence,  aggravated  misconduct,
deliberate  indifference  or  egregious  misconduct  in  the
failure  to  provide  adequate  governmental  services  were
not  actionable  as  a  violation  of due  process,  that  question
need  not  be  answered  in  this  case.  We  believe  that  the

judgment  below  can—and  we  suggest,  should—be  af-
firmed  on  an  equally  sound,  albeit  more  limited  ground
that  child  protective  caseworkers  (and  their  supervisors)
perform  prosecutorial  quasi-judicial  tasks,  as  to  which  a
rule of absolute  immunity’  precludes  money  damages  lia-
bility.

This  Court  has  long  held  that  common  law  immunity
principles  apply  with  equal  force  and  effect  in  42  USC
§ 1983  actions  involving  claims  of constitutional  depriva-
tions.  Jmbler  v  Pachtman,  424  US  409,  424-426  (1976)
(prosecutors);  Stump  v  Sparkman,  435  US  349,  356-357
(1978)  (judges);  Briscoe  v  LaHue,  460  US  325,  329-336
(1983)  (witnesses).  In  each  of  these  cases,  this  Court  has
examined  common  law  immunities  and  fashioned  a  rule
of  section  1983  damages  immunity  comparable  to  the

'This  Court  can  address  the  immunity  issue,  even  though  it was
not  addressed  below,  for  several  reasons.  First,  in  comparable  cir-
cumstances,  this  Court  granted  certiorari  on  the  broader  question  of
whether  negligent  interference  with  an  inmate’s  outgoing  mail  was  ac-
tionable,  but  decided  the  case  on  the  narrower  ground  of  qualified
immunity.  Procunier  v  Navarette,  434  US  555,  559-560  & n  6 (1978).
Second,  the  question  of  whether  immunity  defeats  an  otherwise  ac-
tionable  claim  is reachable  as  a question  incidental  to  those  framed  in
the  certiorari  petition.  See,  Stern,  Gressman  &  Shapiro,  Supreme
Court  Practice  (6th  ed  1986),  § 6.26.  Third,  the  issue  may  be  reached
under  the  “‘mere  enlargement"’  rule  by  which  issues  treated  below
may  be expanded  in this  Court  to  encompass  logically  connected  mat-
ters.  See  Dewey  v  Des  Moines,  173  US  193,  198  (1899);  /ilinois  v
Gates,  462  US  213,  248  (1983)  (White,  J.,  concurring)  (as  to  defenses
to  federal  claims).

US

*This  case  does  not  appear  to  implicate  any  genuine  issues  of
municipal  “‘policy’’,  as  that  term  of art  is discussed  in  the  cases.  See
,  108  S Ct  915  (1988);
City of St.  Louis  v Praprotnik,
Oklahoma  City  v  Tuttle,  471  US  808  (1985);  Pembaur  v  Cincinnati,
475  US  469  (1986).  Because  none  of the  allegations  in  the  complaint
implicate  any  “‘policy”  of  the  two  municipal  entity  defendants,  the
judgment  as  to  them  should  be affirmed  for  failure  to  state  a cause  of
action.  See, Baez  v  Hennessy,
,  No.  87-7834  (2d  Cir
July  27,  1988),  slip opn  at  5069-5074  & n  2.  Accordingly,  this  amicus
brief  focuses  on  the  theory  of  liability  directed  at  caseworkers  and
their  supervisors.

F2d

8

9

common  law  rule.  With  respect  to  absolute  immunity  this
Court  has  consistently  held  that  it is the  nature  of the  gov-
ernmental  actor’s  function,  not  the  employment  title,
which  is  the  dispositive  inquiry.  Butz  v  Economou,  438
US  478,  515  (1978);  Imbler,  424  US  at  424-425.

Applying  the  common  law  standards  which  define  the
tasks  of  quasi-judicial  officers,  the  work  of child  protec-
tive  caseworkers  and  their  supervisors  is entitled  to  abso-
lute  immunity.  Prosecutors  and  administrative  agency
employees  performing  similar  tasks  receive  absolute  im-
munity  for  several  reasons.  In  the  nature  of things,  errors
can  occur  because  decisions  must  be  made,  yet  the  facts
before  the  decisionmaker  may  be  tentative,  sharply  con-
flicting,  or  incomplete.  Prosecutors  and  their  administra-
tive  law  counterparts  need  breathing  space  so  they  may
make  independent,  reasoned  decisions  without  fear  of lia-
bility.  See  Butz,  438  US  at  512-513.  The  same  rationale
has  been  held  to  apply  to  parole  officers  who  make  deci-
sions  in  the  field  with  respect  to  supervision  and/or
whether  to  initiate  parole  revocation  proceedings.  Sellars
v  Procunier,  641  F2d  1295,  1299-1300,  1301-1303  (9th
Cir),  cert.  denied,  454  US  1102  (1981);  Evans  v  Dil-
lahunty,  711  F2d  828  (8th  Cir  1983);  Pate  v  Alabama
Board  of Pardons  and  Paroles,  409  F Supp  478,  479  (MD
Ala  1976),  affd  no  opn,  548  F2d  354  (Sth  Cir  1977);  Tar-
ter  v  State  of New  York,  68  NY2d  511,  510  NYS2d  528,
503  NE2d  84  (1986);  Nelson  v  Missouri  Osteopathic
Foundation,  626  F Supp  604,  607  n  2 (WD  Mo  1985);  see
also,  Hendricks  v  State  of Oregon,  67  Or  App  453,  678
P2d  759,  /v denied,  297  Or  228,  683  P2d  91  (1984)  (same
result  on  rationale  of  ‘“‘discretionary  function’’  exception
to  tort  liability);  C.L.  v  Olson,  143  Wis  2d  701,  422
NW2d  614  (1988)  (same  result  on  rationale  of  ‘‘public  of-
ficer”’  immunity).°

*Moreover,  as  in the  case  of parole  boards  (which  did  not  exist  in
the  Civil  War  era  when  the  predecessor  to  section  1983  was  enacted),
the  courts  will  have  to  reason  by  analogy  to  decide  the  immunity
questions,  since  child  protective  services  caseworkers  were  not  an  or-
ganized  profession  at  the  time  following  the  Civil  War.  See  Butz,  438
US  at  512-517;  Sellars,  641  F2d  at  1298-1299  & n  6.

The  rationale  which  furnishes  absolute  immunity  for
prosecutors  and  parole  officers  applies  with  equal  force  to
child  protective  caseworkers,  because  the  same  type  of
judgmental,  deliberative  decisionmaking  is  involved.
There  is  no  functional  difference  between  the  decision-
making  process  of child  protective  caseworkers,  parole  of-
ficers  and  administrative  agency  prosecutors:  each  meas-
ures  facts,  including  tentatively-determined  facts,  against
generalized  criteria  (e.g.,  constitutional,  statutory  and
regulatory  provisions)  to  decide  whether  or  what  sanc-
tions,  penalties  or  proceedings  to  initiate,  adjudicate  or
terminate.  Each  must  necessarily  make  inherently  subjec-
tive  appraisals  of  the  credibility  and  reliability  of  wit-
nesses  and  suspects.  Each  is equally  capable  of coming  to
the  wrong  conclusion  in  a  particular  case,  because  of
flawed  information,  mistake  in judgment  or  other  error.

Applying  the  teachings  of  this  Court  that  the function
of  the  decisionmaker  is  the  dispositive  factor  (Butz,  438
US  at  513;  Jmbler,  424  US  at  424),  the  majority  of courts
to  consider  the  question  have  held  that  child  protective
caseworkers  (and  their  supervisors)  are  entitled  to  abso-
lute  immunity  from  money  damages  liability.  Walden  v
Wishengrad,  573  F Supp  1115,  1116-1117  (WDNY  1983),
affd,  745  F2d  149,  151-153  (2d Cir  1984)  (absolute  immu-
nity  on  analogy  to  prosecutorial  immunity);  Whelehan  v
County  of Monroe,  558  F Supp  1093,  1098-1099  (WDNY
1983)  (same);  Hennessey  v  State  of Washington,  Dept.  of
Social  and  Healtk  Services,  627  F Supp  137,  140  (ED  Wa
1985)  (same);  Jn  re  Scott  County  Master  Docket,  672  F
Supp  1152,  1171  n  5 (D  Minn  1987)  (citing  list  of  cases);
see  also,  Midamerica  Trust  Co.  v  Moffatt,  158  Ill App  3d
372,  511  NE2d  964,  110  Ill  Dec  787  (Ill App  1987)  (public
officer  immunity  precluded  suit);  but  see,  Doe  v  County
of Suffolk,  494  F Supp  179,  181-182  (EDNY  1980)  (case-
worker  entitled  only  to  qualified  immunity  on  analogy  to
police  officer).  Some  of  the  considerations  recognized  as

10

justifications  for  absolute  immunity  for  these  caseworkers
include  the  fact  that,  like  prosecutors,  these  professionals
must  act  under  serious  constraints  of  time  and  informa-
tion  (Whelehan,  558  F Supp  at  1098);  that  the  threat  of
liability  will  shade  decisionmaking  (Hennessey,  627  F
Supp  at  140;  Whelehan,  558  F  Supp  at  1099);  and
that—because  of  the  potential  for  many  colorable
claims—a  standard  of only  qualified  immunity  would  not
adequately  protect  caseworkers  (Walden,  745  F2d  at  152;
Whelehan,  558  F Supp  at  1099).  We  agree  with  the  rea-
soning  in  these  cases  and  urge  this  Court  to  follow  them
and  to  adopt a rule  of absolute  immunity  for child  protec-
tive  caseworkers  and  their  supervisors.

Neither  prosecutors,  administrative  agency  prosecutors,
parole  officers  nor  child  protective  caseworkers  are,  or
should  be,  exposed  to  money  damages  liability.  Only  ab-
solute  immunity  affords  these  decisionmakers  the  breath-
ing  space  necessary  to  perform  their  often  difficult  tasks
free  of  harassment  or  intimidation.  Butz,  438  US  at
512-513;  Imbler,  424  US  at  422;  Walden,  745  F2d  at
151-153.  Without  absolute  immunity,  these  decision-
makers  will  inevitably  be  hesitant  to  act  decisively,  partic-
ularly  in difficult  cases.  Butz,  438  US  at  512-513:  Jmbler,
424  US  at  422-423.

For  purposes  of  applying  absolute  immunity  for  these
prosecutorial  functions,  it  makes  no  difference  that  the
decisionmaker  made  an  error  of  fact,  law,  or  judgment:
absolute  immunity  bars  all  such  suits.  Prosser  &  Keeton,
Law  of Torts  (Sth  ed  1984),  § 132,  p 1058.  Rather,  it is the
nature  of the function  that  is dispositive  of the  immunity
question.  /mbler,  424  US  at  424-426.

Therefore,  purely  on  the  basis  of the  nature  of the  deci-
sionmaker’s  functions,  child  protective  caseworkers  (and

their  supervisors)  should  be entitled  to  absolute  immunity
from  money  damages.  The  judgment  below  should  be af.
firmed  on  that  basis.

POINT  Il

Due  process  is not  implicated  by alleged  shortcomings
in supervisory  and  monitoring  services  of child  protective
caseworkers,  at  least  where  no  custodial  ‘‘control’’  over
children  is involved.

A.  The  Custodial  Cases  are  not  Controlling

Petitioner  and  amici  in  support  of  petitioner  rely
heavily  upon  cases  recognizing  some  due  process  protec-
tions  against  the  risk  of  injury  in  a  custodial  context
within  an  institution.  Those  cases  are  inapposite.  There  is
no  occasion  in  this  case  for  this  Court  to  address  the
scope  of the  due  process  clause  in the  context  of such  cus-
todial  situations.  This  Court  has  already  addressed  some
of the  issues  in an  institutional  setting  where  residents  (in-
mates  or  patients)  receive  care,  treatment  or  habilitation
under  a  roof  owned  by a  governmental  entity,  and  under
the  direct—and  virtually  constant—supervision  of gov-
ernmental  employees.  In  those  limited  settings,  which  are
not  factually  comparable  to  the  case  at  bar,  this  Court  has
held  some  measure  of liability  exists  for  the  protection  of
inmates  or  mental  patients  from  harm  inflicted  by other
residents  or  staff.  See  Estelle  v  Gamble,  429  US  97,
101-108  (1976);  Youngberg  v  Romeo,  457  US  307,
314-325  (1982);  compare,  Whitley  v  Albers,  475  US 312
(1986)  (shooting  of  prisoner  during  quelling  of  riot  did
not  violate  ban  on  cruel  and  unusual  punishment  or  sub-
stantive  due  process  absent  proof  of  unnecessary  and
wanton  infliction  of  pain).

12

13

The  institutional  cases  stand  on  an  entirely  different
footing  from  non-custodial  situations.  Because  non-
custodial  situations  neither  offer  the  same  access  to  infor-
mation  on the  existence  of  hazards  to  others,  nor  the  op-
portunity  constantly  to  monitor  the  source  of  the  poten-
tial  harm,  there  is no  comparable  ability  of governmental
decisionmakers  to  take  swift  and  decisive  action  to  avert
threatened  harm.  In  an  institutional  setting,  supervisors
can  more  readily  place  potential  aggressors  or  victims  in
separate  housing.  Furthermore,  such  an  institution  by its
nature  is  staffed  around  the  clock.  Moreover,  privacy
rights  of prisoners  and  mental  patients,  while  they  exist  to
some  degree  (Hudson  v  Palmer,  468  US  517,  521,  524
[1984];  Youngberg,  457  US  at  315-316,  324),  are  consid-
erably  less  of  a  barrier  to  oversight  and  monitoring  than
the  parental  rights  in the  social  worker  setting.  Simple  de-
vices  like  locks  on  doors  and  TV  cameras  work  reasonably
effectively  when  the  governmental  actor  owns  and  oper-
ates  the  premises;  considerations  of  privacy,  constitu-
tional  and  otherwise,  positively  preclude  such  prophylac-
tic  devices  in  the  context  of  governmental  oversight  of
third  parties,  such  as  the  parents  of  the  infant  petitioner.

Thus,  precedenis  fashioning  limited  liability  in an  insti-
tutional  context  simply  cannot  form  the  basis  for  liability
in  the  social  services  caseworker  context.  Therefore,  de-
spite  the  reliance  by petitioner  on  Youngberg  and  Estelle,
those  cases  do  not  justify  the  expansion  of  liability  in
these  circumstances.

B.

Special  Factors  in  the  Family  Law  Area

Whatever  may  be  said  of  custodial  situations,  insofar
as  a  custodial  element  of  ‘‘control”’  is  a  factor  favoring
possible  liability,  the  present  case  lacks  even  a  semblance
of  such  control.  Moreover,  here,  there  is an  additional—

and  compelling—countervailing  consideration  which
counsels  strongly  against  the  recognition  of any  liability:
the  required  deference  to  parental  decisionmaking.  This
Court  has  consistently  required  the  States  to  presume  the
right  of  a  parent  to  the  custody  of  his  or  her  child;  to
prove  unsuitability  as  a parent  by clear  and  convincing  ev-
idence;  and  to  defer,  to  a  very  large  degree,  to  parental
decisions  about  the  proper  upbringing  of a child.  See,  e.2.,
Santosky  v  Kramer,  455  US  745,  764  (1982)  (burden  of
proof  to  terminate  parental  rights  is the  high  ‘‘clear  and
convincing  evidence’”’  standard);  Wisconsin  v  Yoder,  406
US  205  (1972)  (right  of Amish  parents  to  keep  their  chil-
dren  out  of public  school  after  the  eighth  grade);  Stanley
v  Illinois,  405  US  645  (1972)  (invalidating  statute  that
conclusively  presumed  that  unwed  fathers  were  unfit  par-
ents  and  thus  incompetent  to  raise  children);  Parham  v
J.R.,  442  US  584,  603  (1979)  (state  may  voluntarily  place
child  in  mental  institution  at  parent’s  request,  with  only
informal  procedures,  without  the  necessity  of adversarial
proceedings  with  representation  for  the  child).  Collec-
tively  these  and  other  well-established  decisions  limit  the
ability  of child  protective  caseworkers  to  take  decisive  ac-
tion  in every  case  which  hindsight  shows  action  may  have
been  warranted.  See  Matter  of Marie  B.,  62  NY2d  352,
477  NYS2d  87,  465  NE2d  807  (1984)  (adjournment  in
contemplation  of dismissal  [‘‘ACD’’]  of child  neglect  pro-
ceedings  could  not  constitutionally  be equated  with  unfit-
ness  of parent,  even  when  parent  voluntarily  consented  to
ACD  and  indisputably  violated  the  terms  of the  ACD);  Jn
re  Enis,  121  Ill  2d  124,  520  NE2d  362,  117  Ili  Dec  201
(1988)  (statute  authorizing  termination  of  parental  rights
merely  upon  actual  proof  of physical  abuse  by a  prepon-
derance  of  evidence  did  not  comport  with  due  process
protections  for  parental  rights).

14

15

The  cases  which  provide  constitutional  protections  in
favor  of  parental  rights  and  parental  custody  demonstrate
that  there  is  no  simple,  automatic  remedy  for  child  pro-
tective  caseworkers  to  employ  any  time  there  is  a  suspi-
cion—or  even  equivocal  evidence—of  child  abuse.  Be-
cause  of  the  procedural  and  substantive  protections  in
favor  of  parents,  child  protective  caseworkers  must  be  re-
alistic  in  their  assessments  of  what  type  of  evidence  will
be deemed  by courts  as  sufficient  to  sustain  allegations  of
child  abuse.  Moreover,  it is highly  doubtful  that  even  the
best-educated  and  properly-motivated  social  services  case-
workers  would  accurai.'’  predict  future  parental  abuse  of
children.  The  picdiction  of  future  human  behavior  is  too
inexact  a  science  for  a  meaningful,  workable  standard  of
care  to  be  articulated,  much  less  to  be  applied  in  each
case.  Tyrell,  Prediction  of Dangerousness,  Vol.  34,  No.  1,
Medical  Trial  Quarterly  24,  31-37  (1987)  (citing  extensive
professional  studies  of  the  problem  in  the  psychiatric
field);  see  also,  Livermore,  Malmquist  &  Meehl,  On  the
Justifications  for Civil  Commitment,  117  U  Pa  L  Rev  75,
84  (1968).*  Thus,  it is inappropriate  to  impose  federalized
tort  liability  in  these  circumstances.

Other  practical  factors  demonstrate  that  the  imposition
of  liability  against  caseworkers  and  their  supervisors
would  be  unfair.  The  caseloads  are  staggering.  To  cite
New  York  as  an  example,  there  are  only  about  2,000  child
protective  services  caseworkers.  Not  counting  existing

“Before  courts  fashion  new  liabilities,  it  is  appropriate  to  con-
sider  whether  a  workable  standard  of  care  exists.  For  example,  be-
cause  it is gencrally  agreed  that  no  consensus  exists  in  the  educational
field  on  the  appropriate  way  to  educate  children,  the  courts  have  uni-
formly  rejected  the  tort  of  educational  malpractice.  Torres  v  Little
Flower  Children’s  Services,  64  NY2d  119,  485  NYS2d  15,  474  NF2d
223  (1984),  cert.  denied,  474  US  864  (1985):  Peter  W.  v  San  Francisco
Unified  School  Dist.,  60  Cal  App  3d  814,  131  Cal  Rptr  854  (1976):
Tubell  v  Dade  County  Public  Schools,  419  So  2d  388  (Fla  App  1982);
Hunter  v  Bd  of  Ed,  292  Md  481,  439  A2d  S82  (1981).

a et ae tes “ete

open  files,  the  numbers  of  new  “‘reports’’  of  child  abuse
or  maltreatment  have  steadily  increased.  There  were
95,626  such  complaints  or  reports  in  1986;  105,059  in
1987;  and  63,608  for  the  first  six  months  of  1988.  Each
caseworker  inevitably  must  make  judgments  about  which
parents  are  more  likely  to  abuse  their  children.  But,  given
the  inability  to  predict  future  human  behavior,  as  well  as
the  strong  presumptions  in  favor  of parental  custody  and
parental  decisionmaking,  it is simply  unfair  to  burden  so-
cial  services  professionals  with  liability  for  the  regrettable
episodes  of child  abuse  that  are  not  prevented.

C.

Prior  Precedents  Do  Not  Support  Liability  And,  In  Fact,
Demonstrate  That  No  Liability  Should  Exist  Here.

A  review  of  the  leading  ‘‘constitutional  tort’’  cases
demonstrates  that,  with  the  possible  limited  exception  of
the  prison  and  mental  health  direct  custody  cases  (supra,
subpoint  ‘‘A’’,  pp  11-12),  the  only  due  process  theory  of
liability  ever  accepted  by this  Court  as  a  justification  for
awarding  damages  has  been  abuse  of  governmental
power.  The  cases  sustaining  liability  have  involved  the  use
of  physical  force  by a  governmental  actor  against  a citi-
zen,  or  the  use  of  a  weapon,  or  some  other  instrumen-
tality.  Yet,  in  every  case  in  which  liability  was  upheld
(either  on  the  pleadings  or  afte.  verdict),  the  central  ele-
ment  of abuse  of governmental  authority  was  present.  See
e.g.,  Home  Tel.  &  Tel.  Co.  v  Los  Angeles,  227  US  278,
287  (1913)  (classic  statement  that  abuse  of  governmental
power  is the  central  inquiry  under  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment);  Monroe  v  Pape,  365  US  167,  172  (1961)  (police
barged  into  home  in  the  middle  of  the  night,  without  a
warrant,  and  forced  the  residents  to  stand  naked  while  the
officers  ransacked  the  house);  Tennessee  v  Garner,  471
US  1, 7-21  (1985)  (police  officer  fatally  shot  unarmed  sus-
pect  fleeing  from  a  burglary  location)  (damages  liability

16

17

justified  under  the  Fourth  Amendment);  see  a/so,  Screws
v  United  States,  325  US  91  (1945)  (it is a  violation  of  the
criminal  law  counterpart  to  42  USC  § 1983  for  police  to
beat  a  prisoner  to  death);  Johnson  v  Glick,  481  F2d  1028,
1033  (2d  Cir)  (Friendly,  J.),  cert.  denied  sub  nom.  John  v
Johnson,  414  US  1033  (1973)  (articulating  oft-cited  test  of
justifications  for  and  measurement  of the  use  of  force  in
action  by  pretrial  detainee  against  police).  These  cases
represent  proper  and  necessary  constitutional  limitations
on  the  affirmative  use  of governmental  power  against  in-
dividuals.  The  abuse  of  power  rationale  operates  as  a
meaningful  deterrent  against  ill-motivated  or  overly-
exuberant  governmental  actors.  The  substantive  due  proc-
ess  principles  governing  those  cases  are  not  implicated
here.

But  recognition  of  the  historic  limitations  which  due
process  imposes  to  prevent  governmental  abuse  of power,
does  not  imply  that  liability  should  be  imposed  on  the
basis  of an  omission  to  furnish  more  or  better  affirmative
services.  There  is a  fundamental  and  dispositive  distinc-
tion  between  these  two  types  of  cases.  These  distinctions
are  ably  described  in  Judge  Posner’s  opinion  in the  case  at
bar,  DeShaney  v  Winnebago  County,  812  F2d  298,
301-304  (7th  Cir  1987),  and  in  the  other  lower  court  deci-
sions  cited  at  page  301.  See  also,  Currie,  Positive  and
Negative  Constitutional  Rights,  53  U  Chi  L  Rev  864
(1986);  McClary  v  O’Hare,  786  F2d  83,  88-89  (2d  Cir
1986)  (action  for  wrongful  death  could  not  be  maintained
as  federal  civil  rights  claim,  where  the  event  was  an  acci-
dent,  neither  the  occurrence  of  which  nor  the  particular
victim  of  which  could  have  been  predicted);  Walker  v
Rowe,  791  F2d  507,  509-512  (7th  Cir),  cert.  denied,  107  S
Ct  597  (1986)  (due  process  does  not  require  a  state  to  pro-
vide  its employees  with  safe  working  conditions  or  to  pro
tect  them  from  murderers).

Petitioner  and  amicus  on  his  behalf  place  considerable,
but  misplaced,  reliance  on  the  common  law  precept  of
‘special  relationships’’.  Citing  the  Third  Circuit  decision
in  Estate  of Bailey  by  Oare  v  County  of  York,  768  F2d
503  (3d Cir  1985),  petitioners  equate  knowledge  of poten-
tial  risk  with  a  constitutional  duty  to  prevent  the  harm.
That  theory  simply  has  no  place  in  the  due  process  juris-
prudence.  The  better  view  is  that  due  process  does  not
embrace  a  governmental  obligation,  to  be  honored  at  the
peril  of  constitutional  tort  damages,  to  protect  citizens
from  crime.  Judge  Posner’s  thoughtful  opinion  for  the
Seventh  Circuit  in  this  case  explains  that  due  process  is
not  a  mandate  for  the  government  to  provide  services  to
its  citizens;  rather,  it  is generally  viewed  as  a  limitation
against  arbitrary  or  abusive  exercises  of  governmental
power.  DeShaney,  812  F2d  at  303-304.  Other  lower  courts
that  have  addressed  this  or  related  concerns  have  similarly
concluded  .  .at  state  tort  remedies,  not  section  1983  law-
suits,  are  the  appropriate  remedy  for  injured  persons.  See,
e.g.,  Harpole  v  Arkansas  Dept.  of Human  Services,  820
F2d  923,  928  (8th  Cir  1987)  (governmental  social  services
caseworker  who  misjudged  ability  and  fitness  of parent  to
care  for  child  with  physical  ailments  was  not  liable  to
grandparent  on  special  relationship  theory);  Wells  v
Walker,  671  F Supp  624,  626-627  (ED  Ark  1987)  (person
injured  by  parolee);  Jones  v  Phyfer,  761  F2d  642  (11th
Cir  1985)  (person  injured  by  furloughed  prisoner);  see
also,  Daniels  v  Williams,  474  US  327,  335-336  (1986)  (al-
ternative  holding  or  dictum  which  rejects  the  common-
law  ‘“‘special  relationship”  test  of  Restatement,  Second,
of  Torts  § 314,  as  a  basis  for  a  constitutional  tort  under
the  due  process  clause).

In  any  event,  the  purported  constitutional  tort  theory
advanced  by petitioner  should  be  rejected  for  the  further
reason  that  ii would  extend  liability  even  beyond  common

18

19

law  bounds.  Section  319  of  the  Restatement,  Second,  of
Torts  permits  a court  to  find  a  ‘‘special  relationship”’  only
if real—rather  than  conjectural  and  indirect—control  (ab-
sent  here)  existed.  See  Sherrill  v  Wilson,  653  SW2d  661,
666-667  (Mo  1983);  Payton  v  United  States,  679  F2d  475,
479-483  (Sth  Cir  1982);  see  also,  Purdy  v Public  Adminis-
trator  of County  of  Westchester,  72  NY2d  1,  1988  WL
53002  (1988)  (operators  of  health  care  facility  did  not
have  enough  ‘“‘control’’  over  conduct  of voluntary  patient
to  justify  liability  for  tort  committed  by  patient  off  the
premises).

Finally,  even  if the  Court  were  inclined  to  recognize  a
duty  in  the  present  context,  we  suggest  that  permitting  a
section  1983  cause  of  action  would  tend  to  convert  this
civil  rights  statute  into  a  broadly-based  federal  tort  rem-
edy.  While  this  Court  has  not  yet  spoken  to  the  issue  of
constitutional  tort  liability  couched  in  terms  of  reckless-
ness  or  gross  negligence  (see  Daniels  v  Williams,  474  US
327,  334  n  3 [1986]),  it has  consistently  declined  to  trans-
form  section  1983  into  a  generalized  federal  tort  law  doc-
trine.  See  Daniels,  474  US  at  329-332  (reiterating  ‘‘abuse
of  power’’  doctrine  as  core-value  of  the  due  process
clause;  holding  that  mere  negligence  of  sheriff’s  deputy
leading  to  injury  to  prisoner  was  not  a  violation  of  due
process);  Davidson  v Cannon,  474  US  344,  347-348  (1986)
(negligent  failure  to  protect  one  inmate  from  attack  by
another,  even  in  the  face  of  notice  of a  specific  threat  by
an  identified  inmate  to  another  identified  inmate,  was  not
actionable  as  a  violation  of  due  process);  Youngberg  v
Romeo,  457  US  307,  317  (1982)  (there  is  no  generalized
constitutional  duty  of  government  to  furnish  substantive
services);  Baker  v McCollan,  443  US  137  (1979)  (incarcer-
ation
of
negligently-caused  misidentification,  was  not  actionable);
Paul  v  Davis,  424  US  693,  699-701  (1976)  (circulation  to

of  wrong  defendant,

on  account

merchants  of  plaintiff’s  name  and  picture,  on  police  list
of  “‘active  shoplifters’’,  even  after  such  charges  had  been
dismissed,  amounted  only  to  a  state  tort  of  defamation,
not  a  violation  of constitutional  due  process);  Martinez  v
California,  444  US  277,  281,  283-285  (1980)  (state  statute
conferring  a  defense  to  potential  state  tort  law  liability,
which  arguably  resulted  in  negligent  release  of dangerous
parolee,  did  not  result  in a deprivation  of due  process).  As
then-Justice  (now  Chief  Justice)  Rehnquist  reasoned  in
Baker,  443  US  at  146:

“Section  1983  imposes  liability  for  violations  of
rights  protected  by the  Constitution,  not  for  viola-
tions  of duties  of care  arising  out  of tort  law.  Rem-
edy  for  the  latter  type  of  injury  must  be  sought  in
state  court  under  traditional  tort-law  principles.
Just  as  ‘[mJedical  malpractice  does  not  become  a
constitutional  violation  merely  because  the  victim
is a  prisoner,’  Estelle  v Gamble,  429  US  97,  106
(1976),  false  imprisonment  does  not  become  a  vio-
lation  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  merely  be-
cause  the  defendant  is a state  official.”’

There  simply  is no  sound  basis  for  federalizing  tort  law.
There  is a jurisprudential  problem  of drawing  the  line  for
liability  at  an  appropriate  and  workable  boundary.  As
then-Judge  (later  Chief  Judge)  Breitel  of  the  New  York
Court  of  Appeals  stated:

‘Beyond  practical  difficulties  there  is a  limit  to
attaining  essential  justice  in this  area.  While  it may
seem  that  there  should  be  a  remedy  for  every
wrong,  this  is an  ideal  limited  perforce  by the  real-
ities  of this  world.  Every  injury  has  ramifying  con-
sequences,  like  the  ripplings  of the  waters,  without
end.  The  problem  for  the  law  is  to  limit  the  legal
consequences  of  wrongs  to  a  controllable  degree.”’

20

Tobin  v  Grossman,  24  NY2d  609,  619,  301  NYS2d  554,
561,  249  NE2d  419,  424  (1969).  Those  observations  are
equally  applicable  here.

There  are  several  practical  problems  that  counsel
against  the  recognition  of  liability  on  theories  of  gross
negligence  or  recklessness.  It would  almost  always  be pos-
sible  to  state  a  cause  of action  on  the  pleadings.  Archie  v
City  of Racine,  847  F2d  1211,  1219  (7th  Cir  1988)  (en
banc)  (extensive  review  of cases,  noting  that  no  workable
distinctions  have  yet  been  offered  in  this  area,  and  con-
cluding  that  ‘‘[a]  line  that  cannot  be  policed  is not  a  line
worth  drawing  in constitutional  law’’).

And,  as  history  has  demonstrated  in the  personal  injury
area,  the  recognition  of a  novel  duty  leads  to  further,  ex-
panded  liability.  Social  services  caseworkers  are  already
being  sued  by parents  who  allege  that  child  removal  pro-
ceedings  were  commenced  on  flimsy  or  inadequate  evi-
dence.  See,  e.g.,  In re  Scott  County  Master  Docket,  672  F
Supp  at  1194,  1198-1199;  see  also,  Smith  v  Eley,  675  F
Supp  1301,  1303-1306  (D Utah  1987)  (action  by custodial
parent  against  county  sheriff  and  deputy  for  assisting  for-
mer  spouse  in gaining  physical  custody  of child).  It is no
exaggeration  to  suggest  that  every  garden-variety  matri-
monial  matter  with  a  fight  over  child  custody  could  be-
come  a  federal  lawsuit.

The  possibilities  of  new  avenues  of  federalized  tort  law
are  merely  unleashed,  and  certainly  not  exhausted,  by the
family  law  area.  Every  other  aspect  of governmental  serv-
ices  could  become  an  inviting  target  for  a  federal  lawsuit
in  the  name  of  due  process.

21

CONCLUSION.

The  judgment  should  be  affirmed.

Dated:  Albany,  New  York
August  9,  1988

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

1.  Whether  the  Due  Process  Clause  imposes  a  duty  on
county  welfare  agencies  and  their  employees  to  provide
child  protective  services  in  an  adequate  manner.

2.  Whether  gross  negligence  or  recklessness  on  the
part  of  county  officials  in  providing  protective  services
can  constitute  a  deprivation  of  rights  under  the  Due
Process  Clause.

3.  Whether  local  government  liability  under  42  U.S.C.
§ 1983  can  be  predicated  on  a  subordinate  official’s  vio-
lation  of government  policy.

4.  Whether  the  individual  respondents  are  protected
by  official  immunity  from  Section  1983  liability  for  a
novel  constitutional  violation.

Supreme  Court  of the United  States
OCTOBER  TERM,  1988

No.  87-154

JOSHUA  DESHANEY,  a  Minor,  by his  Guardian  ad  litem,
and  MELoDY  DESHANEY,

v.

Petitioners,

WINNEBAGO  COUNTY  DEPARTMENT  OF  SOCIAL  SERVICES,
WINNEBAGO  COUNTY,  ANN  KEMMETER  and
CHERYL  STELSE,

Respondents.

On  Writ  of Certiorari  to the  United  States
Court  of Appeals  for  the Seventh  Circuit

BRIEF  OF THE
NATIONAL  ASSOCIATION  OF  COUNTIES,
COUNCIL  OF  STATE  GOVERNMENTS,
U.S.  CONFERENCE  OF  MAYORS,
NATIONAL  CONFERENCE  OF  STATE  LEGISLATURES,
NATIONAL  LEAGUE  OF  CITIES,  AND
INTERNATIONAL  CITY  MANAGEMENT  ASSOCIATION
AS  AMICI  CURIAE  IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS

INTEREST  OF  THE  AMICI  CURIAE

The  amici,  organizations  whose  members  include  state,
county,  and  municipal  governments  and  officials  through-
out  the  United  States,  have  a  compelling  interest  in  legal
issues  that  affect  state  and  local  governments.

2

Amici  are  concerned  that  a  reversal

in  this  case
would  vastly  expand  the  liability  of  state  and  local  gov-
Petitioners  seek  to
ernments  under  42  U.S.C.  § 1983.
hold  the  County  and  individual  child  welfare  profes-
sionals  liable  because  they  did  not  prevent  the  severe
abuse  of  a  child  by  his  father,  the  child’s  lawful  cus-
todian.  Amici  and  their  members  are  deeply  concerned
about  Joshua  DeShaney  and  other  victims  of  the  sense-
less  cruelty  of  child  abuse.  The  brutality  of  Randy  De-
Shaney  and  the  severity  of  Joshua’s  injuries,  however,
should  not  be  permitted  to  obscure  the  underlying  legal
issue:  whether  the  County  is  constitutionally  liable  for
the  father’s  crime  because  a  social  worker  failed  to  take
some  action  that  might  have  prevented  injuries  to  the
child.

Private  violence,  no  matter  how  severe  or  how  tragic
the  consequences,  is  still  private  violence.
Petitioners’
claims  cannot  be  upheld  unless  the  Court  abrogates  im-
portant  limitations  on  the  liability  of  local  governments
uhder  Section  1983,  as  well  as  the  official  immunity  of
local  government  officials.  Further  expansion  of  Section
1983  liability  to  include  claims  far  removed  from  the
core  concerns  of  the  Due  Process  Clause  would  divert
ordinary  state  law  tort  claims  to  the  federal  courts  and
impose  substantial  additional  costs  on  state  and  local
governments.

Amici  are  also  concerned  that  a  reversal  would  result
in  a  mandate  under  Section  1983  empowering  the  federal
courts  to  address  child  abuse  and  other  family  law
issues  that  are  the  recognized  province  of  state  and
local  governments.  Child  abuse  is  one  of  the  grave  prob-
lems  of  our  society  that  governments  at  all  levels  are
grappling  with  today.  The  discretion  of  those  govern-
ments  to  address  child  abuse,  or  similar  social  problems,
should  not  be  controlled  by  a  federal  common  law  of
social  work  of  curtailed  by  the  threat  of  litigation  every
time  a  tragedy  occurs.

3

Amici  submit  that  the  decision  below  is  correct.

Be-
cause  this  Court’s  decision  will  have  a  direct  effect  on
matters  of prime  importance  to  amici  and  their  members,
amici  submit  this  brief  to  assist  the  Court  in  its  resolu-
tion  of the case.’

STATEMENT

Petitioners  Joshua  and  Melody  DeShaney  brought  this
action  under  42  U.S.C.  § 1983,  claiming  that  they  were
deprived  of  their  liberty  without  due  process  of  law  by
respondents  because  social  worker  Ann  Kemmeter;  her
supervisor,  Cheryl  Stelse;  her  employer,  the  Winnebago
County  Department  of  Social  Services;  and  Winnebago
County  failed  to  intervene  effectively  in  a  case  of  sus-
pected  child  abuse.  Finding  no  constitutional  basis  for
these  claims,  the  district  court  entered  summary  judg-
ment  in  favor  of  respondents.
The  court  of  appeals
affirmed.

Joshua  DeShaney  was  born  in  Wyoming  in  1979.

In
December  1980,
a  Wyoming  court  awarded  Randy  De-
Shaney  custody  of  Joshua  following  Mr.  DeShaney’s  di-
vorce  from  Melody  DeSnaney.  Randy  DeShaney  there-
after  moved  with  his  son  to  Winnebago  County,  Wiscon-
sin.  In  March  1984,  while  in  his  father’s  custody,  Joshua
was  brutally  beaten  by  his  father  and  suffered  critical
brain  injuries.

Respondent  Ann  Kemmeter’s  involvement  with  Randy
and  Joshua  DeShaney  began  in  January  1983,  when
Randy’s  girlfriend  Marie  DeShaney  (Joshua’s  half-aunt)
brought  Joshua,  covered  with  bruises,  to  Theda  Clark
Regional  Medical  Center.  Marie  DeShaney  stated  that
another  child  had  inflicted  the  bruises,  but  the  emergency
room  staff  suspected  child  abuse  and  alerted  respondent
Winnebago  County  Department  of  Social  Services  (the
“Department”’).

'  The  parties’  letters  of  consent,  pursuant  to  Rule  36  of  the  Rules

of the  Court,  have  been  filed  with  the  Clerk.

4

5

By  the  end  of  the  day,  the  Department  had  obtained
an  order  placing  Joshua  in  the  temporary  custody  of  the
Medical  Center.  The  Department  convened  a  “child  pro-
tective  team”  consisting  of  a  pediatrician,  a  psycholo-
gist,  a  police  detective,  a  lawyer  for  respondent  Winne-
bago  County  (the  “County”),  and  respondents  Kem-
meter  and  Stelse.  The  county  attorney  concluded  that
there  was  insufficient  evidence  of  child  abuse  to  retain
Joshua  in  county  custody;  and  he  was  therefore  returned
to  his  father.  As  the  court  of  appeals  observed,  there  is
no  evidence  that  the  Department  was  reckless  in  making
this  decision.  Pet.  App.  42-43.

Although  the  child-protection  case  was  closed  three
weeks  later,  respondent  Kemmeter  made  periodic  visits
to  the  DeShaney  household  and  maintained  a  file  on
Joshua.  Ms.  Kemmeter  was  aware  that  Joshua  had  suf-
fered  repeated  suspicious  injuries,  although,  in  each
instance,  Randy  DeShaney  offered  an  innocent  explana-
tion.
Ms.  Kemmeter’s  last  visit  occurred  on  March  7,
1984,  when  Randy  DeShaney  informed  her  that  Joshua
had  fainted  in  the  bathroom  several  days  earlier  for  no
Ms.  Kemmeter  did  not  ask  to  see
apparent  reason.
Joshua.

The  following  day,  Randy  DeShaney  beat  his  son  so
severely  that  the  child  suffered  grave  neurological  dam-
age  and  was  hospitalized.
Respondents  subsequently
placed  Joshua  in  a  foster  home  for  profoundly  retarded
children.  Randy  DeShaney  was  convicted  of  child  abuse
and  sentenced  to  prison.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

On  its  face,  the  question  whether  a  county  social
welfare  agency  adequately  implemented  the  State’s  child
welfare  program  is  a  question  of  state  law  for  state
courts.
Petitioners  cannot  succeed  in  constitutionalizing
this  question  without  vastly  expanding  the  liability  of
local  government  agencies  and  officials.  Reversal  in  this

case  would  require  that  the  Court  radically  alter  at  least
four  principles  that  limit  such  liability  under  Section
1983.

First,  the  Due  Process  Clause  acts  to  restrict  the
powers  of  government,  not  to  impose  upon  it  affirmative
duties  to  provide  social  welfare  services.  Petitioners  ask
the  Court  to  impose  on  government  welfare  agencies  an
affirmative  constitutional  duty  to  prevent  child  abuse
when  they  are  aware  of  a  risk  to  a  child.  The  Constitu-
tion  provides  no  source  for  this  affirmative  duty,  partic-
ularly  in  this  case,  where  Joshua  DeShaney  was  not  in
the  County’s  custody,  but  in  the  legal  and  physical  cus-
tody  of his  abusive  father.

Even  if  petitioners’  claims  could  be  grounded  in  the
Constitution,  recognition  of  those  claims  would  entail  an
extraordinary  and  unwarranted  intrusion  into  areas  of
fundamental  state  concern.
This  Court  has  recognized
the  primacy  of  the  States  in  dealing  with  issues  of  fam-
ily  law,  and  the  federal  courts  are  ill-suited  to  make
these  decisions.
Nor  is  it  sound  policy  to  subject  the
decisions  of  county  welfare  workers  to  the  ex  post  facto
scrutiny  of  the  federal  courts.  Finally,  there  is  no  prin-
cipled  basis  for  distinguishing  child  welfare  services
from  other  protective  services.  As  a  result,  reversal  in
this  case  could  constitutionalize  analogous  duties  of  other
government  employees,  including  parole  officers,  police
officers,  and  paramedics.

allegedly  reckless

Second,  even  if  petitioners  could  show  a  constitutional
right  to  official  protection  from  private  violence,  they
could  not  pursue  a  claim  of  deprivation  based  on,  at
most,
The  language  and
jurisprudence  of  the  Due  Process  Clause  limit  violations
to  conduct  that  can  fairly  be  called  intentional.  To  hold
otherwise  would  permit  the  negligence  claims  prohibited
by  Daniels  v.  Williams,  474  U.S.  327  (1986),  to  be  pur-
sued  at  least  to  trial—and  frequently  to  judgment—
under  the  guise  of  gross  negligence  or  recklessness.

conduct.

Third,  petitioners  can  recover  against  the  County  and
the  Department  only  on  the  theory  of  respondeat  superior
liability,  which  has  been  repeatedly  disavowed  by  the

Court.
fractions  of  County  policy  as  the  basis  for  their  due
process  claims.  They  cannot  also  assert  that  the  same
policy,  if  followed,  would  have  caused  a  due  process  vio-
lation,  as  required  by  Monell  v.  Department  of  Social

Services,  436  U.S.  658  (1978).
precluded  from  establishing  liability  under  Monell  be-
cause  (a)  the  County’s  policy  is  constitutional,  a  point
