respondents  from  liability.

Finally,  the  doctrine  of  official  immunity  protects  the
individual
Child  welfare
workers,  as  executive  officials,  are  entitled  to  qualified
immunity  for  discretionary  actions  taken  in  good  faith.
Because  the  individual  respondents  could  not  have  an-
itcipated  that  they  had  a  federal  constitutional  duty  to
prevent  child  abuse,  their  conduct  fell  within  the  scope
of qualified  immunity.

ARGUMENT

I.  THE  DUE  PROCESS  CLAUSE  IMPOSES  NO  DUTY
TO  PROVIDE  SOCIAL  SERVICES  IN  AN  ADE-
QUATE  MANNER.

The  Due  Process  Clause  does  not  impose  affirmative
duties  on  the  States.  It  is,  instead,  “a  restraint  upon  the
The  Slaughter-house  Cases,  83
power  of  the  States.”
U.S.  (16  Wall.)  36,  80  (1873).  The  Civil  Rights  Cases,
109  U.S.  3,  11  (1883),  recognized  that,  under  the  Due
Process  Clause,  “[i]t  is  State  action  of  a  particular
character  that  is  prohibited.”  In  holding  that  only  state
action  violates  the  Constitution,  the  Court  specifically
observed  that  “individual  invasion  of  individual  rights  is
not  the  subject-matter  of  the  amendment.”
Jd.  The
fundamental  understanding  of  the  Constitution  as  a  lim-
itation  on  the  power  of  the  States,  not  a  mandate  of  af-

7

firmative  duties,  continues  to  the  present.
See,  e.g.,
Kadrmas  v.  Dickinson  Public  Schools,  108  S.  Ct.  2481,
2489  (1988);  Youngberg  v.  Romeo,  457  U.S.  305,  317
(1982);  Harris  v.  McRae,  448  U.S.  297,  318  (1980).

The  government’s  “duty”  to  protect  members  of  the
public  at  large  from  private  violence  is  one  such  af-
firmative  obligation  not  found  in  the  Constitution.
As
petitioners  and  their  amici  recognize,  the  Constitution
does  not  impose  such  a  generalized  duty  on  the  govern-
Pet.  Br.  at  12-13;  Brief  Amicus  Curiae  of  the
ment.
American  Civil  Liberties  Union,  et  al.
(hereinafter
“ACLU”  and  “ACLU  Br.’”),  at  9.  See  Martinez  v.
California,  444  U.S.  277,  284-85  (1980).
At  least  in
the  absence  of  physicai  custody  of  the  claimant,  as  in
Youngberg  v.  Romeo,  457  U.S.  at  317  (involuntarily  com-
mitted  mental  patients),  there  is  no  basis  under  the  Con-
stitution  for  requiring  state  and  local  governments  to
provide  any  services,  regardless  of  what  state  law  may
require.

It  is undisputed  that  respondents  did  not  injure  Joshua
DeShaney.  Joshua  was  critically  injured  by  his  father,
and  petitioners  do  not  contend  that  Randy  DeShaney’s
brutal  aes  can  be  directly  attributed  to  respondents.
Thus,  petic.oners’  claim  depends  on  the  existence  of  an
affirmative  obligation  of government  under  the  Due  Proc-
ess  Clause*  to  protect  suspected  victims  of  child  abuse
from  the  violence  of their  own  parents,  an  obligation  that
cannot  be  squared  with  the  Court’s  consistent  view  of
that  Clause  as  a  limitation  on  power  and  not  an  affirma-
tive  grant  of  rights.  Moreover,  although  petitioners  have
identified  certain  characteristics  of  child  protective  serv-
ices  that  distinguish  them  from  other  government  pro-

2  Our  reference  throughout  is  to  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  although  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment  would  subject  federal  agencies  and  officials  to
the  same  liability  if  petitioners’  arguments  were  accepted.

8

9

grams,  none  of  these  distinctions  provides  a  constitu-
tional  basis  for  thrusting  the  federal  courts  into  the
province  of  state  and  local  governments  or  burdening
those  governments  with  costly  new  obligations  to  provide
adequate  social  services.

A.  The  “Special  Relationship”  Doctrine  Does  Not  Fur-
nish  A  Constitutional  Basis  For  Imposing  Affirma-
tive  Duties  On  State  And  Local  Governments.
To  subject  state  and  local  governments  to  new  affirma-
tive  duties,  petitioners  rely  on  court  of  appeals  decisions
creating  a  doctrine  of “special  relationships.”  Under  these
cases,  certain  factors  can  compel  governments  to  provide
protective  social  services  to  potential  victims  of  private
violence.  These  factors  are  primarily  (a)  the  expressed
desire  to  protect  a  particular  class  of  individuals:  (b)
knowledge  of  the  victim’s  plight;  and  (c)  legal  custody.
See  Pet.  Br.  at  14,  citing  Jensen  v.  Conrad,  747  F.2d
185,  194-95  n.11  (4th  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  470  U.S.  1052
(1984) .°

As  applied  to  this  case,  none  of  the  “special  relation-
ship”  factors  have  constitutional  significance,  whatever
their  significance  under  state  law.  The  government’s

*  This  line  of  cases  includes  Taylor  v.  Ledbetter,  818  F.2d  791
(llth  Cir.  1987)  (en  banc),  pet.  for  cert.  filed,  56  U.S.L.W.  3290
(U.S.  Sept.  25,  1987)  (No.  87-521):  Estate  of  Bailey  v.  County  of
York,  768  F.2d  503  (3d  Cir.  1985);  Doe  v.  New  York  City  Dep’t
of  Social  Services,  649  F.2d  134  (2d  Cir.  1981),  cert.  denied,  464
U.S.  864  (1983).

Like  the  courts  below,  other  circuits  have  rejected  the  doctrine.
See  Harpole  v.  Arkansas  Dep't  of  Human  S: rvices,  820  F.2d  923
(8th  Cir.  1987);  Washington  v.  District  of  Columbia,  802  F.2d
1478,  1481-82  (D.C.  Cir.  1986);  Estate  of  Gilmore  v.  Buckley,  787
F.2d  714  (1st  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  107  S.Ct.  270  (1986).  Harpole
involved  facts  virtually  identical  to  those  presented  here.

*  Massachusetts,  for  example,  has  developed  a  doctrine  of  special
relationships  under  its  common  law,  without  any  reliance  on  the  Due
Process  Clause.  See  A.L.  v.  Commonwealth,  521  N.E.2d  1017  ( Mass.
1988).

generalized  desire  to  protect  a  class  of  individuals  has  no
constitutional  relevance  whatsover.  Because  that  desire
will  be  present  in  all  social  programs  intended  to  pro-
tect  citizens,  it  provides  no  meaningful  limit  on  the
kinds  and  extent  of  affirmative  obligations  that  would
Moreover,  this  Court  has  repeatedly  held
be  created.
that  a  State’s  deprivation  of  state-created  rights  does  not
violate  the  Due  Process  Clause  unless  those  rights  amount
See,
to  a  constitutionally  protected  property  interest.
e.g.,  Baker  v.  McCollan,  443  U.S.  137  (1979);  Paul  v.
Davis,  424  U.S.  693  (1976);  Screws  v.  United  States,
325  U.S.  91  (1945).  A  fortiori,  a  mere  expressed  in-
tent  to  protect  a  class—even  if  accompanied  by  a  proce-
dure  to  facilitate  protection—does  not  create  a  constitu-
tional  right  to  protection.  To  hold  otherwise  would  con-
vert  every  government  social  service,  and  possibly  every
statement  of policy,  into  a  constitutional  right.

The  second  factor,  knowledge  of  the  victim’s  plight,
also  cannot  impose  on  government  an  affirmative  duty  to
protect.  Surprisingly,  the  authority  cited  for  this  factor
is the  Court’s  failure  to  reach  this  question  in  Martinez  v.
California,  444  U.S.  277  (1980).
See,  e.g.,  Estate  of
Bailey  v.  County  of  York,  768  F.2d  503,  510  (3d  Cir.
1985).  Martinez  held  that  the  California  Parole  Board
could  not  be  liable  under  the  Due  Process  Clause  for  a
murder  committed  by a  parolee  five  months  after  his  re-
lease  on  parole.  In  limiting  the  scope  of  its  ruling,  the
Court,  noting  that  the  Board  was  unaware  of  the  danger
to  a  particular  victim,  stated  that,  “[w]e  need  not  and  do
not  decide  that  a  parole  officer  could  never  be  deemed  to
‘deprive’  someone  of  life  by  action  taken  in  connection
with  the  release  of  a  prisoner  on  parole.”  444  U.S.  at
286  (emphasis  added).  Some  lower  courts  have  none-
theless  taken  this  considered  refusal  to  speculate  as  an
established  principle  supporting  imposition  of  liability.

10

11

Analytically,  knowledge  of  a  risk  to  an  identified  per-
son  is  of  no  greater  constitutional  significance  than  a
generalized  intent  to  benefit  the  class  to  which  that  per-
son  belongs.  The  suggestion  that  knowledge  creates  the
duty  is  an  impermissibly  circular  proposition;  the  con-
stitutional  duty  defines  the  weight,  if  any,  to  be  given
to knowledge.

Moreover,  to  hold  that  knowledge  of  a  risk  is  sufficient
to  create  a  constitutional  duty  would  extend  the  reach  of
the  Due  Process  Clause  in  a  revolutionary  manner.  Many
government  agencies,  especially  police  departments,  fire
departments,  and  child  welfare  agencies,  compile  files
identifying  potential  victims  at  risk.  Because  of  their
limited  resources,  these  agencies  are  daily  asked  to  assign
priorities  among  these  risks.  Liability  based  on  knowledge
of  a  risk  would  render  any  failure  by  these  agencies  to
provide  adequate  protective  services  actionable.  But  the
Due  Process  Clause  has  never  been  thought  to  mandate
supervision  by  the  federal  courts  of  decisions  made  in
the  face  of potential  risks.

The  final  factor,  legal  custody,  is  not  present  in  this
case.°  Were  it  present,  it  would  not  require  a  different
result.  Legal  custody  is  insufficient  to  impose  affirmative
federal  constitutional  duties  on  the  government,  unless  it
is  accompanied  by  physical  custody  that  impinges  on  the
liberty  interests  of  the  citizen.  Legal  custody  is  insuffi-

5  The  initial  decision  granting  Randy  DeShaney  custody  of  his
son  was  made  in  Wyoming,  not  Wisconsin.  The  child  was  in  tem-
porary  state  custody  in  Wisconsin  only  during  the  period  of  his
hospitalization  in  January  1983.  At  that  time,  the  hospital  con-
vened  a  team  of  doctors  and  social  workers  that  determined  that
there  were  insufficient  grounds  for  terminating  Randy  DeShaney’s
parental  rights.  The  court  of  appeals  held  that  this  decision  was
not  reckless  (Pet.  App.  42-43),  and  there  is  no  evidence  that  this
determination  was  even  negligent.

cient  because  state  law  determines  legal  custody  and  its
legal  significance.
For  example,  the  State  may  retain
legal  custody  of  an  orphan  who  is  placed  with  foster
parents.  Yet,  if  the  State  relinquishes  parental  responsi-
bilities  to  the  foster  parents  under  state  law,  no  liberty
interest  of  the  child  is  impinged  once  the  child  has  been
properly  placed.  See  Lehman  v.  Lycoming  County  Chil-
dren’s  Services  Agency,  458  U.S.  502,  510-11
(1982).
In  Lehman,  the  Court  noted  that,  for  purposes  of  federal
habeas  corpus  under  28  U.S.C.  § 2254,  foster  children
“are  in  the  ‘custody’  of  their  foster  parents  in  essentially
the  same  way,  and  to  the  same  extent,  other  children  are
in  the  custody  of  their  natural  or  adoptive  parents.  ..  .
[T] hey  suffer  no  unusual  restraints  not  imposed  on  other
children.”

This  Court  has  recognized  that  certain  responsibilities
may  attach  under  the  Due  Process  Clause  where  the
State  has  physical  custody.*  In  Youngberg  v.  Romeo,  457
U.S.  305  (1982),  a  suit  by  an  involuntarily  committed
mental  patient,  the  Court  explicitly  premised  the  State’s
duties  on  its  preexisting  deprivation  of  the  patient’s
liberty :

As  a  general  matter,  a  State  is  under  no  constitu-
tional  duty  to  provide  substantive  services  for  those
within  its  border.  When  a  person  is  institutional-
ized—and  wholly  dependent  on  the  State—it  is  con-
ceded  by  petitioners  that  a  duty  to  provide  certain
services  and  care  does  exist,  although  even  then  a

®  Even  the  ACLU  recognizes,  although  it  rejects,  “the  attractive-
ness  of  [physical]  custody  as  a  bright  line  for  determining  the
limits  of  liability.”  ACLU  Br.  at  44.  In  fact,  physical  custody  is
a  necessary  but  not  a  sufficient  condition  for  recovery.  The  Four-
teenth  Amendment  does  not  constitutionajze  all  common  law  duties
owed  to  a  person  in  custody.  See  Daniels  v.  Williams,  474  U.S.  327,
332  (1986).

12

13

State  necessarily  has  considerable  discretion  in  de-
termining  the  nature  and  scope  of  its  responsibilities.

Id.  at  317  (citations  omitted).?  See  also  Hutto  v.  Finney,
437  U.S.  678  (1978)  (Eighth  Amendment  case  involving
state  prisoners).

These  cases  depend  on  the  concept  that  government,
when  it  deprives  a  person  of  liberty  and  the  inherent
capacity  for  self-protection,  assumes  the  responsibility  to
minimize  other  intrusions  into  protected  liberty  interests.
Absent  that  preexisting  deprivation,  there  is  no  consti-
tutional  basis  for  imposing  affirmative  duties.

Petitioners  assert  that  it  would  be  appropriate  to
impose  affirmative  constitutional  duties  in  this  case  be-
cause  respondents’  authority  under  Wisconsin  law  “pre-
empted”  the  rescue  efforts  of  others.  Their  assertion  is
simply  incorrect.  Neither  respondents  nor  the  Wisconsin
Children’s  Code  prevented  neighbors,  police,  or  petitioner
Melody  DeShaney,  Joshua’s  mother,  from  investigating  or
reporting  his  condition.
In  fact,  the  Children’s  Code
mandates  that  the  sheriff  or  police  must  immediately  in-
vestigate  allegations  of  child  abuse  upon  request.
Wis.
Stat.  Ann.  § 48.981(3)(b)  (1987).
Petitioners  identify
no  evidence  that  Melody  DeShaney  was  aware  of  respond-
ents’  involvement  with  Joshua  or  that  anyone  else  failed
to  report,  investigate,  or  restrain  Randy  DeShaney  be-
cause  they  relied  on  the  Department.

Even  if there  were  evidence  of  reliance  on  the  Depart-
ment,  petitioners  could  not  show  a  deprivation  of  consti-
tutional  rights  because  they  have  no  entitlement  to  the

‘The  ACLU  cites  Ingraham  v.  Wright,  430  U.S.  651  (1977).
which  limited  corporal  punishment  of  school  children.
Although
Ingraham  recognized  a  liberty  interest  in  freedom  from  govern-
ment  intrusions  on  personal  security  (id.  at  673),  the  remedy  was
to  restrain  action  by  the  State,  not  to  require  the  State  to  prevent
the  violence  of  others.  Moreover,  school  children  are  compelled  by
law  to  attend  school  and  are  in  the  physical  custody  of  the  State
while  there.

intervention  of  others.  There  is  no  constitutional  right
to  government  social  services  (Harris  v.  McRae,  448
U.S.  at  317-18),  much  less  to  the  services  of  private  ‘citi-
zens.  And  even  if  state  Jaw  imposes  a  duty  of  care  on  a
would-be  rescuer  who  preempts  the  rescue  efforts  of
others,  there  is  no  basis  for  transforming  this  common
law  principle  into  a  constitutional  right.

In  the  end,  petitioners  seek  to  create  a  constitutional
violation  out  of  respondents’  shortfall  in  achieving  their
own  aspirations,  namely  to  prevent  child  abuse.
The
Due  Process  Clause  does  not  transform  those  aspirations
into  constitutional  duties.*

B.  Petitioners’  Claims  Threaten  An  Extreme  And  Un-
warranted  Intrusion  Into  The  Traditional  Role  Of
The  States.

To  hold  that  the  Due  Process  Clause  imposes  widespread
affirmative  duties  “would  mark  a  drastic  change  in  our
understanding  of  the  Constitution.”
Harris  v.  McRae,
448  U.S.  at  318.
Because  there  is  no  constitutional
foundation  for  these  duties,  it  is  not  necessary  to  demon-
strate  why  the  harm  to  state  and  local  governments  of  a
reversal  would  drastically  outweigh  any  social  good  from
such  a  decision.  Yet,  the  weakness  of  petitioners’  claim
is  underlined  by  identifying  only  the  most
obvious
effects  of  reversal  on  the  administration  of  state  social
services.”

*In  this  Court,  petitioners  argue  for  the  first  time  that  they  had
an  “entitlement”  under  the  Due  Process  Clause  to  the  social  serv-
ices  mandated  by  the  Wisconsin  Children’s  Code,  Wis.  Stat.  Ann.
§ 48.01  et  seq.  (1987).  Because  this  procedural  due  process  argu-
ment  (see  Bogrd  of  Regents  v.  Roth,  408  U.S.  564,  570  (1972))  was
not  presented  for  decision  in  the  lower  courts,  it  cannot  furnish  a
basis  for  reversal  here.  See,  e.g.,  Hoover  v.  Ronwin.  466  U.S.  558.
574  n.25  (1984).  In  any  event,  petitioners  cannot  transmogrify  Roth
into  a  constitutional  entitlement  to  social  services  without  render-
ing  the  entire  spectrum  of  state  social  services  subject  to  federal
regulation.

®  Even  when  a  deprivation  of  a  protected  liberty  interest  has  been
shown,  the  Court  will  compare  the  burdens  imposed  on  the  State

14

15

1.  Family  law  concerns  are  matters  for  the  States

to  resolve.

The  law  of  family  relationships  remains,  as  it  always
has  been,  a  concern  of  state  law.  See,  e.g.,  Thompson  v.
Thompson,  108  S.  Ct.  513,  520  (1988);  Rose  v.  Rose,  107
S.  Ct.  2029  (1987);  Pennoyer  v.  Neff,  95  U.S.  (5  Otto)
714,  734-35  (1878).  Many  of  the  decisions  affecting
family  relationships  are  made  by specialized  state  family
“d juvenile  courts  that  deal  with  these  issues  on  a  daily
asis.

Allowing  petitioners  to  bring  their  claims  under  the
Due Process  Clause  would  inescapably  turn  federal  courts
into alternative  and  highly  inappropriate  domestic  rela-
tions  forums.  Petitioners’  claims  would  turn,  at  trial,  on
whether  respondents  provided  adequate  child  welfare  serv-
ices  to  Joshua  DeShaney.  Yet,  as  the  Court  has  repeatedly
observed  (most  recently  in  Thompson  v.  Thompson,  108
S.  Ct.  at  520),  the  federal  courts  “have  little  expertise  to
resolve”  family  law  issues..
It  is  doubtful  that  federal
judges  ever  could  gain  sufficient  expertise.  No  federal
judge  would  want,  let  alone  see,  sufficient  numbers  of
cases  to  be  able  to  place  the  behavior  of  a  given  social
worker  or  parent  in  context.
;
explained  below:

ea

Balancing  the  rights  of  parents  with  those  of  their
children  is  a  task  as  difficult  as  it  is  delicate,  and
we  doubt  that  it  will  be  performed  better  under  the
eyes  of  federal  courts  administering  constitutional
law  than  by  the  state  judicial  and  administrative
authorities.  “The  federal  courts
...  are  not  local
institutions,  they  do  not  have  staffs  of social  workers
and  there  is  too  little  commonality  between  family
law  adjudication  and  the  normal  responsibilities  of
federal  judges  to  give  them  the  experience  they
would  need  to  be  able  to  resolve  domestic  disputes

As  the  court  of  a

and  the  individual  before  finding  a  constitutional  violation.
Youngberg  v.  Romey,  457  U.S.  at  322-23.

with  skill  and  sensitivity.”
F.2d  489,  492  (7th  Cir.  1982).

Lloyd  v.  Loeffler,  694

Pet.  App.  44.

The  ACLU  has  inadvertently  emphasized  the  difficulty.
To  support  the  doubtful  proposition  (discussed  below)
that  federal  liability  would  not  unduly  affect  the  actions
of  social  workers,  it  sets  forth  “generally  accepted  stand-
ards  in  child  protection”  that  the  federal  courts  would
be  called  on  to  apply.  ACLU  Br.  at  36;  see  id.  at  37-38
(identifying  sources  of  “authoritative  standards”).  But
to  apply  these  standards  in  the  difficult  cases  that  come
to  trial,  the  federal  courts  would  have  to  create  a  federal
common  law  governing  social  services.

Thompson  demonstrates  a  related  problem.  In  Thomp-
son,  the  petitioner  filed  suit  in  federal  court  under  the
Parental  Kidnapping  Prevention  Act,  28  U.S.C.  § 17384,
to  overturn  an  adverse  state  court  custody  decisien.  Hold-
ing  that  no  right  of  action  could  be  implied,  the  Court
indicated  that  the  federal  courts  should  not  resolve  “tra-
ditional  domestic-relations  inquiries,”  including  whether
108  S.  Ct.
“the  child  has  been  abandoned  or  abused.”
at  520  n.4.  Petitioners’  claims  would  require  the  federal
courts  to  make  precisely  this  type  of  inquiry.  Moreover,
because  injunctive  relief  is  available  under  Section  1983,
federal  courts  could  be  asked  to  override  the  custody
decisions  of  state  agencies  at  the  time  those  decisions  are
made,  as  well  as  to  second-guess  them  after  they  are
made,  as  in this  case.*®

10  The  availability  of  injunctive  relief  could  lead  to  even  greater
federal  intrusion.  One  author  cited  by  the  ACLU  has  asserted  the
desirability  of  “structural  injunctions”  to  reform  the  provision  of
social  services  to  children.  See  Mushlin,  Unsafe  Havens:  The  Case
for  Constitutional  Protection  of  Foster  Children  from  Abuse  and
Neglect,  23  Harv.  Civ.  R.-Civ.  L.  L.  Rev.  199  (1988).  Such  injunc-
tions  would  deprive  state  and  local  governments  of  their  freedom
to  determine  for  themselves  how  the  difficult  problem  of  child  abuse
should  be addressed.

16

17

There  simply  is  no  imperative  for  pushing  these  cases
into  federal  court.''  While  any  incident  of  child  abuse  is
disturbing,  there  is no  evidence  that  victims  of  child  abuse
are  disfavored  under  state  law  or  victims  of  bias  in  state
courts.”  The  detailed  Wisconsin  statutory  scheme  upon
pain petitioners  rely  is  persuasive  evidence  to  the  con-
rary.

In  reality,  intervention  by the  federal  courts  could  well
undermine  the  quality  of  government  social  services.  The
standards  governing  social  work  would  be  determined  ex-
clusively  by  an  adversary  process,  never  intended  to  de-
termine  sound  policy  and  easily  manipulated  by  clever
counsel.  The  decisions  of  the  federal  courts  would  not
only  dictate  the  standards  a  social  worker  must  follow
at  her  peril,  but  would  lead  to  new  bureaucratic  require-
ments  so  that  the  social  worker  could  document  her  ad-
herence  to  such  standards.
The  prospect  of  unlimited
federal  court  judgments  against  individual  social  work-
ers  and  their  agencies  may  well  ensure  adherence  to
whatever  federal  standards  are  decreed,  but  will  also
deter  social  workers  from  acting  on  their  “honest  judg-
ment”  that  the  best  interests  of  the  child  require  other
action.  Lehman  v.  Lycoming  County  Children’s  Services
Agency,  458  U.S.  at  514  n.18."

In  this  case,  the  only  purported  inadequacy  of  state  court
remedies  identified  by  petitioners  is  Wisconsin’s  $50,000  limitation
on  damage  awards  against  respondents.
See  Wis.  Stat.  Ann.
§ 893.80(3)  (1987).  Petitioners,  however,  have  not  challenged  the
constitutionality  of this  limitation.  If the  limitation  is  valid,  no  pur-
pose  is  served  by  allowing  petitioners  to  use  Section  1983  to  cir-
cumvent  the  statute.

The  Equal  Protection  Clause  serves  to  prevent  state  and  local
governments  from  refusing  to  provide  services  for  invidious  rea-
sons  such  as  racial  discrimination.
See,  e.g.,  Brown  v.  Board  of
Education,  347  U.S.  483  (1954).
.
- For  example,  if petitioners  prevail  here,  it  would  be  an  intrepid
social worker  who  would  fail  to  insist  on  seeing  a  child  on  any  home
visit,  even  if the  refusal  to  accept  a  parent’s  assertion  that  the  child

The  court  of  appeals  detailed  an  additional  dilemma
(Pet.  App.  44-45).  Child  welfare  workers  already  face
potential  liability  under  state  and  possibly  federal  law  if
they  improperly  remove  a  child  from  a  parent’s  custody.
See,  e.g.,  Lossman  v.  Pekarske,  707  F.2d  288,  290  (7th
Cir.  1983);  Drabek  v.  Sabley,  142  N.W.2d  798  (Wis.
Social  work  is  sufficiently  difficult  without  the
1966).
threat  of  liability  whether  the  child  remains  with  his
parent  or  is  removed  from  the  parent’s  custody.  There
is  certainly  no  federal  constitutional  basis  for  creating
an  incentive  to  terminate  parental  custody  which,  unlike
state  social  services,  is  a fundamental  constitutional  right.

2.  The  federal  courts  should  not  reallocate  expendi-

tures  in state  social  welfare  budgets.
Constitutional  liability  for  allegedly  incompetent  social
work  could  diminish  the  availability  as  well  as  the  qual-
ity  of  state  social  services.  Given  the  disturbing  facts
of  any  child  abuse  case,  million-dollar  verdicts  against
government  agencies  and  officials  are  likely.  These  ver-
dicts,  as  well  as  litigation  costs  incurred  regardless  of
the  verdict,  would  necessarily  direct  state  expenditures
away  from  the  provision  of  services.  Such  reallocation  is
a  policy  matter  for  state  legislatures,  not  the  federal
courts.'*

The  threat  of  litigation  costs  diminishing  the  avail-
ability  of  social  services  is  real.  Amici  regretfully  ac-
knowledge  that  child  abuse  is  all  too  prevalent  in  this
country,  despite  the  best  efforts  by  state  and  local  gov-
ernments  to  prevent  it.  The  AC’.U  notes  the  distressing

was  sick  or  sleeping  would  destroy  the  social  worker’s  relationship
with  the  parent.

14  Indeed,  the  ACLU’s  arguments  (Br.  at  6-7)  suggest  that  state
and  local  governments  would  be  better  advised  to  eliminate  such
services  entirely;  for  although  they  are  concededly  not  constitu-
tionally  compelled,  they  are  said  to  furnish  a  basis  for  liability  once
undertaken.

18

19

Statistic  that  there  were  2,250,000  reports  of  suspected
child  abuse  or  neglect  in  1987.  ACLU  Br.  at  44.  In  many
cases,  there  will  be  indications  that,  in  hindsight,  might
have  alerted  a  social  worker  to  possible  abuse.
Espe-
cially  given  petitioners’  contention  that  nonintentional
(i.e,  reckless  or  grossly  negligent)  behavior  is  sufficient
to  bring  a  Section  1983  action  to  a  jury,  there  is  every
reason  to  expect  claims  like  petitioners’  to  proliferate.

3.  Reversal  would  create  a  generalized  constitu-
tional  right  to government  protective  services.

The  federal  courts  are  not  underutilized.  The  proposed
(although  deficient)
constitutional  basis  of  petitioners’
claims  cannot  be  confined  to  cases  of  suspected  child  abuse
in  any  principled  way.  Where  a  government  agency  or
official  is  aware  that  an  individual  citizen  is  at  risk  and
responds  in  a  manner  judged  in  hindsight  not  to  be  ade-
quate,  new  classes  of  cases  will  be  brought—in  volume—
under  Section  1988.  Among  the  most  obvious  categories
of  defendants  are  police  officers,  parole  officers,  para-
medics,  prison  guards,  and  state-employed  physicians,  in-
cluding  psychiatrists.

It  is  not  speculation  that  these  cases  will  be  brought.
Beginning  with  Martinez  v.  California,  444  U.S.  277
(1980),  a  suit  against  the  California  Parole  Board,  these
cases  have  been  filed  even  without  encouragement  from
this  Court.”  In  the  end,  the  federal  courts  will  be  com-
pelled  to  create  a  common  law  governing  the  actions  of
these  additional  classes  of  defendants  as  well  as  child
abuse  caseworkers.

Foster  care  services  would  be  particularly  vulnerable
to  this  type  of  litigation.  Two  circuits  have  already  held

15  See,  e.g.,  Archie  v.  City  of  Racine,  847  F.2d  1211  (7th  Cir.
1988)
(en  banc)  (paramedic  service);  Wideman  v.  Shallowford
Community  Hospital,  Inc.,  826  F.2d  1030  (11th  Cir.  1987)  (ambu-
lance  drivers);  Commonwealth  Bank  &  Trust  Co.  v.  Russell,  825
F.2d  12  (3d  Cir.  1987)  (prison  officials).

that  these  suits  are  cognizable.*®  Reversal  in  this  case,
where  the  child  resided  with  his  natural  parent,  would
necessarily  imply  corresponding  duties  for  government
agencies  continuously  to  monitor  foster  care  agencies and
foster  parents.
The  financial  consequences  of  liability
could  substantially  impair,  if  not  destroy,  foster  care
services  in  many  States.

In  sum,  a  decision  recognizing  petitioners’  claims  under
the  Due  Process  Clause  would  be  far-reaching  and  per-
nicious.  There  is  neither  a  constitutional  foundation  nor
a  practical  justification  to  warrant  a  massive  federal
intrusion  into  areas  that  are  quintessentially  the  province
of the  States.

Il.  THE  COURT  SHOULD  NOT  TRIVIALIZE  THE  DUE
PROCESS  CLAUSE  BY  PERMITTING  SECTION
1983  ACTIONS  TO  PROCEED  TO  TRIAL  ON  ALLE-
GATIONS  OF  GROSS  NEGLIGENCE  OR  RECK-
LESSNESS.

The  Due  Process  Clause  prohibits  “deliberate  decisions
of  government  officials  to  deprive  a  person  of  life,  liberty,
or  property.”
Daniels  v.  Williams,  474  US.  327,  331
It  is  intended  to
(1986)
prevent  “abuse  of  power  by the  government.”  Id. at 330.
Daniels  relied  heavily  on  Justice  Powell’s  opinion  in  Par-
ratt  v.  Taylor,  451  U.S.  527  (1981),  which  had  con-
cluded:

(emphasis  in  the  original).*

A  “deprivation”  connotes  an  intentional  act  denying
something  to  someone,  or  at  the  very  least,  a  delib-
erate  decision  not  to  act  to  prevent  a  loss.  The  most

16  See  Taylor  v.  Ledbetter,  818  F.2d  791  (11th  Cir.  1987)  (en

banc),  pet.  for  cert.  filed,  56  U.S.L.W.  3290  (U.S.  Sept.  25,  1987)
(No.  87-521);  Doe  v.  New  York  City  Dep’t  of  Social  Services,  649
F.2d  134  (2d  Cir.  1981),  cert.  denied,  464  U.S.  864  (1983).

17  Of  course,  the  Court  need  not  reach  this  question  if  it  finds
that  petitioners  have  not  been  deprived  of  an  interest  protected
by the  Due  Process  Clause.

20

21

reasonable  interpretation  of  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment  would  limit  due  process  claims  to  such  active
deprivations.

Id.  at  548  (concurring  opinion).
See  also  Estelle  v.
Gamble,  429  U.S.  97,  105  (1976)
(“deliberate  indiffer-
ence”  to  prisoner’s  serious  illness  or  injury  sufficient  to
constitute
cruel  and  unusual  punishment  under  the
Eighth  Amendment) ; Wolff  v.  McDonnell,  418  U.S.  539,
558  (1974)  (“the  touchstone  of  due  process  is  protection
of  the  individual  from  arbitrary  action”);  cf.  West  v.
Atkins,  108  S.Ct.  2250,
(Sealia,  J.,  con-
curring)  (“deliberate  indifference”  by  physician  causing
physical  harm  to  prisoner  constitutes  the  deprivation
of  liberty  without  due  process).

(1988)

“Abuse  of  power”  strongly  suggests  that  intentional
malfeasance  is  required  to  state  a  claim  of  constitutional
stature."  At  the  very  least,  it  requires  such  deliberate
indifference  that  intent  can  be  inferred.  For  example,  in
Estelle  v.  Gamble,  429  U.S.  97  (1976),  the  allegations  of
deliberate  indifference  held  sufficient  to  state  an  Eighth
Amendment  claim  concerned  the  indifference  “by  prison
guards  in  intentionally  denying  or  delaying  access  to
medical  care  or  intentionally  interfering  with  the  treat-
ment  once  prescribed.”  Jd.  at  104-05  (emphasis  added).
Claims  based  on  negligence  do  not  suffice.  See  Daniels,
supra;  Davidson  v.  Cannon,  474  U.S.  344  (1986).
Nor,
in  our  view,  do  claims  of  gross  negligence  or  even  reck-
lessness."
Permitting  the  trial  of  such  allegations  in

'*  The  cases  cited  in  the  text  all  arose  in  the  prison  context,
where  the  State,  having  assumed  physical  custody,  might  well  be
held  to  a  higher  standard  of  care  because  of  its  assumption  of
responsibility  for  personal  security.  See  pages  10-12,  supra.

'  Neither  Smith  v.  Wade,  461  U.S.  30  (1983),  nor  Justice
O’Connor’s  dissent  in  City  of  Springfield  v.  Kibbe.  107  S.  Ct.  1114,
1121  (1987),  casts  any  light  on  the  state  of  mind  necessary  to
show  a  violation  of  the  Due  Process  Clause.  Smith  v.  Wade  was

federal  court  would  eviscerate  the  Court’s  repeated  deci-
sions  that  garden-variety  tort  actions  against  state  offi-
See,  e.g.,
cials  must  be  brought  under  state
Daniels;  Davidson  v.  Cannon,  474  U.S.  344  (1986);  Paul
v.  Davis,  424  U.S.  693  (1976).

law.

Gross  negligence  is  simply  negligence  “with  the  ad-
dition  of  a  vituperative  epithet”  (Wilson  v.  Brett,  152
Eng.Rep.  737,  739  (Ex.  1843)
(Rolfe,  B.))};  and  reck-
lessness,  also  an  aggravated  form  of  negligence,  can  be
defined  as  lack  of  due  care  in  the  presence  of  an  ob-
vious  risk  of  harm.”  Like  simple  negligence,  gross  neg-
ligence  and  recklessness  are  insufficient  to  show  the
abuse  of  power  required  by  Daniels.*'  Especially  where,
as  here,  injury  results  from  violence  inflicted  by  private
persons,  an  Official’s  failure  to  prevent  it  is  not  an
“abuse  of  power,”  unless  that  failure  is  deliberate  and
knowing.”

concerned  with  the  state  of  mind  necessary  to  support  punitive
damages  under  Section  1983.
Smith  v.  Wade  explicitly  distin-
guished  the  state  of  mind  necessary  for  punitive  damages  under
the  statute  from  the  state  of  mind  necessary  to  show  the  underly-
ing  constitutional  violation.

461  U.S.  at  50.

Similarly,  Justice  O’Connor’s  opinion  in  Kibbe  dealt  only  with
the  state  of  mind  sufficient  to  establish  a  policy  under  Monell  v.
Department  of Social  Services,  436  U.S.  658  (1978)  (discussed  infra,
Part  III),  without  discussing  whether  reckless  conduct  can  give
rise  to  a  due  process  violation.

20  See  W.  Prosser  &  W.  Keeton,  The  Law  of  Torts  213,  214  (5th

ed.  1984).

21  See  Hogan  v.  City  of  Houston,  819  F.2d  604,  605  (5th  Cir.
(gross  negligence  insufficient);  Washington  v.  District  of

1987)
Columbia,  802  F.2d  1478,  1481  (D.C.  Cir.  1986)  (same).

22  It  is  not  necessary  to  decide  in  this  case  that  no  behavior  that
could  be  called  “reckless”  can  ever  constitute  a  due  process  viola-
tion.  For  example,  there  may  be  instances  of  criminal  recklessness

22

Unintentional  actions,  even  when  they  are  grossly
negligent,  do  not  violate  the  Due  Process  Clause.  Gross
negligence  is  not,  as  petitioners  and  the  ACLU  argue,
analogous  to  “deliberate  indifference.”  Estelle  v.  Gamble
equated  deliberate  indifference  with  intentional  conduct
(see page  20, supra).

Holding  allegations  of  gross  negligence  or  recklessness
sufficient  to  state  a  claim  would,  in  practical  consequence,
overrule  Daniels.  Daniels  noted  that  the  difference  be-
tween  intent  and  negligence  “is  abundantly  clear.”  474
U.S.  at  335.  The  distinction  between  negligence  and  reck-
lessness,  let  alone  gross  negligence,  is  not.**  Many  tort
lawyers  routinely  plead  negligence  in  the  alternative  as
gross  negligence  or  recklessness.  The  complaint  in  this
case  ({/ 44,  J.A.  11)  is an  example.  If  it  is held  to  state
a  claim,  plaintiffs  will  simply  satisfy  the  pleading  re-
quirements  and  proceed  in  federal  court.  Moreover,  be-
cause  allegations  of gross  negligence  or  recklessness  raise
questions  of  fact,  they  are  ordinarily  not  subject  to  dis-
missal  or  summary  judgment  prior  to trial.

that  are  tantamount  to  intent  and  could  therefore  require  a  dif-
ferent  analysis.  Cf.  Whitley  v.  Albers,  475  U.S.  312  (1986).  In
this  case,  however,  Ms.  Kemmeter  at  the  most  “recklessly”  failed
to  act  effectively  to  prevent  the  abuse  of  Joshua  DeShaney.
Petitioners  have  adduced  no  evidence  that  she  (or  any  other  re-
spondent)  intended  that  harm  come  to  Joshua  or  was  deliberately
indifferent  to  his  plight.  Ms.  Kemmeter’s  repeated  visits  and  her
own  notes  (e.g.,  “I  continue  to  have  a  great  deal  of  concern  over
the  welfare  of  Joshua”  (J.A.  118))  are  strong  evidence  to  the
contrary.

*8 Petitioners  certainly  do  not  explain  the  distinctions  between
negligence  and  gross  negligence  or  recklessness.  They  assert  that
respondents’  alleged  “misconduct”  was  “beyond  mere  negligence”
(Pet.  Br.  at  33),  but  they  offer  no  further  characterization  or  ex-
planation  of  their  “beyond  mere  negligence”  standard.

23
Ultimately,  the  concepts  of  gross  negligence  and  reck-
lessness  cannot  appropriately  be  applied  to  the  services
provided  by  government  officials.  Those  officials  are  re-
quired,  with  limited  resources,  to  evaluate  risks  on  a
daily  basis.  A  police  desk  sergeant  must  decide  the  order
in  which  the  police  will  respond  to  emergency  calls.
Overloaded  caseworkers  must  decide  the  relative  serious-
ness  of  all  cases  of  suspected  child  abuse.  Hindsight  can
make  any  decision  look  reckless  when,  as  here,  tragic
If  gross  negligence  or  recklessness
consequences  ensue.
claims  are  actionable  under  the  Due  Process  Clause,  the
day-to-day  functioning  of  state  and  local  government  of-
ficials  will  become  a  constant  source  of  Section  1983  liti-
gation.  Plainly,  that  Clause  was  never  intended  to  en-
compass  the  routine  administration  of  state  and  local
governments.  See  Davidson  v.  Cannon,  474  U.S.  at  347-
48.  Given  the  Court’s  concern  not  to  trivialize  the  Due
Process  Clause,  it  is  neither  necessary  nor  sensible  to
divert  a  potentially  limitless  volume  of  ordinary  tort
cases  from  state  to federal  court  for  trial.

III.  NO  POLICY  HAS  BEEN  SHOWN  TO  ESTABLISH
THE  COUNTY’S  LIABILITY  UNDER  MONELL  v.
DEPARTMENT  OF  SOCIAL  SERVICES.

Even  if petitioners  could  establish  first,  that  petitioners
had  a  constitutional  right  to  government  intervention  to
prevent  Randy  DeShaney’s  abuse  of  Joshua,  and  second,
that  respondents’  actions  (or  nonactions)  deprived  them
of  that  right  in  a  manner  proscribed  by  the  Due  Process
Clause,  the  principles  of  local  government  liability  under
Section  1983  first  delineated  in  Monell  v.  Department  of
Social  Services,  436  U.S.  658  (1978),  would  preclude  any
recovery  from  the  County  and  the  Department.”

24  Although  the  Monell  issue  was  not  addressed  by  the  courts
below,  the  Court  may  rely  on  grounds  not  previously  raised  to  af-
firm  the  judgment  of  the  lower  court.  Dandridge  v.  Williams,  397
U.S.  471,  574  n.6  (1970).  Because  petitioners’  own  submissions
establish  the  absence  of  the  requisite  policy  under  Monell,  a  re-
mand  for  further  consideration  of  this  issue  is  not  necessary.

24

25

In  Monell,  the  Court  held  that  local  government  agen-
cies  could  be  held  responsible  under  Section  1983  for  the
acts  of  their  employees  only  if  those  acts  were  executed
pursuant  to  a  “policy  or  custom.”  436  U.S.  at  694.  The
policy  or  custom  must  be  “  ‘the  moving  force  of  the  con-
stitutional  violation.’”  Polk  County  v.  Dodson,  454  U.S.
312,  326  (1981),  citing  Monell,  436  U.S.  at  694.  Implicit
in  Monell  is  the  recognition  that  local  governments  should
not  routinely  face  damage  claims  for  alleged  constitu-
tional  violations  by  their  employees  unless  such  bodies
are  directly  responsible  for  the  violations.

In  this  case,  the  County’s  operative  policy  was  “to
seek  out  and  prevent  child  abuse.”  Pet.  Br.  at  19.  As
petitioners  themselves  explain,  the  Department’s  Child
Protection  Unit  was  created  “in  response  to  the  declared
policy  of  Wisconsin  law  mandating  specific  efforts  to
protect  endangered  children  and  intervene  on  their  be-
half.”
Jd.  at  4.  Petitioners’  claims  against  Ms.  Kem-
meter  thus  rest  in  large  measure  on  her  alleged  failure  to
follow  the  Department’s  guidelines.  See  id.  at  7  (failure  to
visit  frequently);  id.  at  8  (failure  to  inform  natural
mother  of risk).

Petitioners  cannot  have  it both  ways.  Having  asserted
that  their  due  process  deprivation  resulted  from  Ms.  Kem-
meter’s  failure  to  follow  the  County’s  policy,  they  cannot
also  claim  (as  they  must  under  Monell)  that  the  policy
itself  caused  the  violation  of  their  constitutional  rights.
Even  if petitioners  could  show  that  County  policy  played
a  causal  role  in  any  constitutional  violation,  liability
against  the  County  and  the  Department  would  be  barred
by  the  rule  of  Monell,  as  explained  in  later  cases.  First,
as  the  plurality  opinion  articulated  in  City  of  St.  Louis
v.  Praprotnik,  108  S.Ct.  915,  926  (1988),  a  local  govern-
ment  “cannot  be  held  liable  under  § 1983  unless  [the
claimant]  proved  the  existence  of  an  unconstitutional

municipal  policy.”  *  Here,  petitioners  do  not dispute  the
constitutionality  of  the  County’s  avowed  policy,  and,  like
the  claimant  in  Praprotnik,  they  have  adduced  no  evi-
dence  that  departure  from  that  policy  was  either  au-
thorized  by  County  policymakers  or  sanctioned  by  wide-
spread  custom  or  practice.

Second,  petitioners’  only  evidentiary  basis  for  the
County’s  liability  is  Ms.  Kemmeter’s  failure  to  intervene
in  this  single  case.  Because  Monell  precludes  respondeat
superior  liability  for  her  alleged  shortcomings,  petitioners
cannot  pursue  their  claims  against  the  County  and  the
Department  based  on  Ms.  Kemmeter’s  violations  of  their
policies.  In  City  of  Oklahoma  City  v.  Tuttle,  471 US.
808  (1985),  the  plurality  concluded  that  a  single  incident
of  misfeasance  by  a  subordinate  cannot  provide  the  sole
basis  for  inferring  a  policy  under  Monell.  Id.  at  824.
To  find  the  County  or  the  Department  liable  on  the  facts
of  this  case,  given  the  absence  of  direct  evidence  that
they  were  at  fault,  would  impose  respondeat  superior
liability  in another  guise.

IV.  THE  INDIVIDUAL  RESPONDENTS  ARE _ EN-

TITLED  TO  OFFICIAL  IMMUNITY.

Just  as  the absence  of a policy  defeats  petitioners’  claims
against  the  County  and  the  Department,  official  immunity

25  As  amici  have  urged  the  Court  in  a  brief  amicus  curiae  sub-
mitted  in  City  of  Canton  v.  Harris,  No.  86-1088,  this  conclusion
follows  logically  from  Monell  and  its  progeny,  City  of  Oklahoma
City  v.  Tuttle,  471  U.S.  808  (1985)  ;  Pembaur  v.  City  of Cincinnati,
475  U.S.  469  (1986);  and  Praprotnik.  Our  brief  in  City  of  Canton
is  being  served  on  the  parties  to  this  case  simultaneouly  with  this

brief.

26  Because  Ms.  Kemmeter  was  not  a  municipal  policymaker,  the
County  respondents  cannot  be  liable  for  the  single  alleged  episode
of  incompetence  on  which  their  claims  are  based.  See  Praprotnik,
108  S.Ct.  at  924;  Pembaur  v.  City  of Cincinnati,  475  U.S.  469  (1986).

27

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  the  judgment  of the United
States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Seventh  Circuit  should  be
affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted,

BENNA  RUTH  SOLOMON

STATE  AND  LOCAL
LEGAL  CENTER

Counsel  of Record  for the

Amici  Curiae

26

protects  the  individual  respondeats  from  liability.”  Ac-
cordingly,  the  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals  should  be
affirmed  even  if a  constitutional  violation  were  shown.

It  requires  little  analysis  to  show  that  the  individual
respondents  cannot  be  held  liable  in  this  case.  Child  wel-
fare  workers,  as  executive  officials  performing  discretion-
ary  functions,  have  routinely  been  held  entitled  to qualified
official  immunity.  See  Austin  v.  Borel,  830  F.2d  1356,
1363  (5th  Cir.  1987);  Jensen  v.  Conrad,  747  F.2d  185,
195  (4th  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  470  U.S.  1052
(1984)  ;
Donald  M.  v.  Matava,  668  F.Supp.  703,  707-13  (D.  Mass.
1987).

It  is  now  settled  that  qualified  immunity  protects  offi-
cials  from  suit  “insofar  as  their  conduct  does  not  violate
clearly  established  statutory  or  constitutional  rights  of
which  a  reasonable  person  would  have  known.”  Harlow
v.  Fitzgerald,  457  U.S.  800,  818  (1982).
Given  the
novelty  of  petitioners’  claims,  Ms.  Kemmeter  and  Ms.
Stelse  could  not  have  known  that  they  had  an  affirmative
constitutional  duty  to  a  child  residing  with  his  father.
There  was  no  published  case  on  point  until  more  than  a
year  after  the  alleged  due  process  violation  (see  Estate
of  Bailey  v.  County  of  York,  768  F.2d  508  (8d  Cir.
1985)  ),  and  no  clear  rule  has  emerged  even  today.  See
Harpole  v.  Arkansas  Dep’t  of Human  Services,  820  F.2d
923  (8th  Cir.  1987)
(no  constitutional  duty  to  prevent
child  abuse  where  the  child  resided  with  his  parent).
Because  the  individual  respondents’  conduct  unquestion-
ably  fell  within  the  scope  of qualified  immunity,  they  were
entitled  to  summary  judgment  on  this  Separate  ground.

*7  Although  respondents  pleaded  official  immunity  in  their  an-
swer  (Answer,  Affirmative  Defense  No.  2,  J.A.  28),  and  raised
this  issue  in  the  district  court,  neither  the  district  court  nor  the
court  of  appeals  considered  official  immunity  in  ruling  in  favor  of
respondents.  As  with  Monell’s  limitations  on  municipal  liability,
official  immunity  is  offered  in  support  of  the  judgment  below.  See
Dandridge  v.  Willi:
1s,  397  U.S.  at  475  n.6.
